Water Corrosion Mechanisms
By Paul R. Puckorius
his article addresses the cause and con-
trol of corrosion of metals commonly
found in cooling water systems. The
pipes, tubes, coils, water boxes, and cool-
ing tower must be protected from corrosion.
Each cooling water system has a unique combi-
nation of metals, water flow, temperatures, and
makeup water quality that must be considered
before selecting a corrosion control program.
The first step in selecting this treatment is to
identify all metals in the system. Then, we must
know the limitations of the corrosion inhibitors.
Finally, cost- effective corrosion inhibitors can
be selected. However, first we need to under-
stand corrosion.
In the corrosion cell illustrated in Figure 1, a neutral
iron atom loses two electrons and becomes an iron ion,
Fe++, in solution in the electrolyte. The electrons flow
through the metallic path to the cathode and back into
the electrolyte. They combine with two positively charged
hydrogen ions in the electrolyte. The resulting molecule
of hydrogen escapes as a gas.
If this highly simplified diagram told the entire story,
the system would reach equilibrium and corrosion would
stop. This condition is called passivation. However, ad-
ditional reactions take place. The removal of hydrogen
ions from the electrolyte at the cathode (producing hy-
drogen molecules) leaves an excess of hydroxyl (OH–)
ions. These hydroxyl ions react with the iron ions in the
solution forming ferrous hydroxide.
Fe++ + 2 OH– → Fe(OH)2
The ferrous hydroxide Fe(OH)2 dissolves, allowing the
corrosion reaction to continue.
In oxygenated water, as in open systems like cooling
towers, further reactions occur. Dissolved oxygen reacts
with ferrous hydroxide at the anode to produce ferric
hydroxide, which is less soluble than ferrous hydroxide.
At the cathode, dissolved hydrogen reacts with dissolved
oxygen to form water. A more comprehensive picture of
galvanic corrosion in the presence of oxygen is the same
as Figure 1 with these reactions:
Fe++ + 2 OH– → Fe(OH)2
4 Fe(OH)2+ O2 + 2 H2O → 4 Fe(OH)3
O2 + 2 H2O + 4 e– → 4 OH–
4 H+ + 4 OH–- → 4 H2O
Figure 2 illustrates this phenomenon.
Galvanic Corrosion
The galvanic series (Table 1) is a table that allows engi-
Paul R. Puckorius, president of Puckorius & Associates,
Inc., has more than 35 years experience in water treat-
ment and troubleshooting. His company is an indepen-
dent water and wastewater management consulting firm
that provides technical consulting and does not sell chemi-
cals or equipment.
T
Electrical Nature of Corrosion
Corrosion is the dissolution of materials of construc-
tion by their environment. It includes rusting of iron,
rotting of wood, weathering of stone, bearing wear, and
even the vaporizing of a satellite re-entering the atmo-
sphere. In this article, we consider only aqueous corro-
sion of metal. This is always associated with an electro-
chemical reaction, as shown in Figure 1.
Electricity flows between metal areas through a solu-
tion. Corrosion takes place where electrons leave the
metal and ions enter the solution. This area is called the
anode. The area where electrons return to the metal is
called the cathode. Besides the conducting solution
(electrolyte) and the two electrodes (anode and cath-
ode), there must be an electron path to complete the
electric circuit. The metal structure itself may provide
the electron path, or the circuit may be completed by
physical contact between the metals. The simplest ex-
ample is galvanic corrosion of two dissimilar metals. Fa-
miliar examples are steel pipe screwed into a copper
fitting and mild steel tubes in a brass tube sheet of a heat
exchanger.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, May 1999. © Copyright 1999 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-

form without permission of ASHRAE.
5 8
P R AC T I C A L G U I D E
M ay 19 9 9
Water Treatment
neers to predict which metal will corrode when differ-
ent metals are in contact. In most cases, the metal that is
higher in the table will corrode, and the metal that is
lower in the table will be protected.
The galvanic series predicts only whether corrosion can
occur. Corrosion rates are determined by the following:
9 What metals are electrically coupled.
9 The relative anodic to cathodic surface areas.
9 A larger cathode—A higher corrosion rate.
9 A smaller cathode—A lower corrosion rate.
9 A large anode—General sheet type corrosion.
9 A small anode—Pitting type corrosive attack.
It is not always necessary to have two dissimilar metals
in the system. Many metals, particularly iron, can have
anodic and cathodic areas at the same time. The poten-
tial differences between anodic and cathodic areas in
the same metal are the result of grain boundaries, grain
orientation, differential grain size, differential thermal
treatment, surface roughness, scratches, differential strain,
etc. When immersed in an electrolyte, current flows from
the anodic to the cathodic areas and corrosion results.
The anode and cathode are the same as for the galvanic
cell. The metal itself forms the electron path. Where the
corrosion in the galvanic cells in a single metal is more
or less uniform, this action tends to cause pitting.
Even a completely homogenous metal can corrode if
it is immersed in a nonhomogenous liquid. The classical
concept of a concentration cell is shown in Figure 3. In
general, the specimen in the more concentrated solu-
tion is anodic to the specimen in the more dilute solu-
tion. A common type of concentration cell occurs in crev-
ices or dead areas such as those under deposits. This
phenomenon is known as crevice corrosion and is illus-
trated in Figure 4.
The crevice above, for example, hinders the diffusion
of oxygen into the wetted area of the overlap of two
riveted members. The result is high oxygen in the body
of water and low oxygen in the crevice, causing a con-
centration cell. If the metal involved is steel, the area of
low concentration is anodic; if copper, it is cathodic.
Because an electrochemical cell is necessary for corro-
sion, the logical method of preventing corrosion is to de-
stroy the cell. One method of doing this is to impose a
nonconducting barrier between the metal and the
electrolyte.
Corrosion Control Concepts
The rate of attack by circulating cooling water on steel
is a function of temperature and pH. It increases with
temperature and depression of pH.
Corrosion protection follows two rules:
1. High pH promotes scale and inhibits corrosion.
2. Low pH inhibits scale and promotes corrosion.
Figure 5 illustrates the effect of pH on the corrosion
rate of unprotected mild steel in water.
Corrosion control relies on a barrier between the metal
and the corrosive medium. Barriers can be calcium car-
bonate or calcium phosphate scale that forms within the
system. These relatively thick barriers have the serious
drawback of impeding heat transfer. On the other hand,
chemical inhibitors can be used to form a thin protective
film. The film can be metallic or non-metallic.
Steel surfaces have both anodic and cathodic areas in a
ratio of about 80:20. The traditional view is that the ferrite
phase acts as the anode and the cementite phase acts as the
Figure 1: Typical corrosion cell.
Figure 2: Electrochemical reaction.
Figure 3: Differential corrosion.
Figure 4: Crevice corrosion.
M ay 19 9 9
P R AC T I C A L G U I D E
5 9
Practical Guide
cathode. However, the situation is seldom so simple.
If the anode could be isolated from the electrolyte by
some sort of film, corrosion could be controlled. In 1899,
engineers found that certain oxidizing materials, such as
nitrites, would inhibit corrosion. This inhibition is the re-
sult of an adherent, insoluble oxide film, 30 to 50 A° thick,
that forms on the anode surface by a reaction between the
iron going into solution, the oxidizing inhibitor, and/or
oxygen in solution. There has been much controversy as
to the composition of these films, so they will be referred
to as gamma iron oxide films (GFe2O3 or mayhenite).
Most oxidizing agents, including oxygen, are anodic
inhibitors. Steel will not corrode in salt water covered
with oxygen at several atmospheres pressure. Anodic in-
hibitors oxidize the anodic base metal and form a thin,
tightly adherent, oxide layer that interrupts the electri-
cal circuit.
The most efficient anodic inhibitor is the pertechnate
ion. Unfortunately, for the users of circulating water, it is
highly radioactive.
The most popular anodic inhibitor was the chromate
ion. It is inexpensive, very soluble, and quite easy to
use. Although chromate is no longer used because of
environmental concerns, it is the model anodic inhibi-
tor. Other anodic inhibitors include nitrates, molybdates,
tungstates, orthophosphates, silicates, ferricyanides,
persulfates, borates, and benzoates. To be effective, high
concentrations of these oxidizing inhibitors are required.
If the chromate concentration dropped much below 500
ppm, general corrosion was controlled, but severe pit-
ting attack could occur.
Assume the system shown in Figure 6 is corroding at
the rate of 10 mils per year. Now add enough anodic
inhibitor (about 50 ppm chromate) to convert 90% of
the anodic surface to iron oxide, as shown schematically
in Figure 7. The current flow remains essentially the same;
the amount of iron removed remains essentially the same,
but iron is removed from only 10% of the anode. There-
fore, the corrosion rate on this reduced area is 100 mils
per year, a severe pitting condition.
Pitting is usually more severe on the cool end of an
exchanger. A rule of thumb states that the rate of a chemi-
cal reaction doubles for every 10°F increase in tempera-
ture. Thus, the iron oxide film would form faster, tighter,
and more completely on the hot end of the exchanger.
To prevent pitting, a protective film should be depos-
ited in the cathode areas as well as in the anode areas.
In the early 1940s, engineers found that a mixture of
sodium polyphosphate and sodium nitrite had greater in-
hibitive properties than would have been predicted by the
sum of their individual inhibitive effects. Lacking a better
term, such mixtures were called “synergistic” inhibitors.
Today, the preferred term is cathanodic inhibitors.
Referring back to Figure 6, we see that simultaneously
with the oxidation reaction taking place at the anode, a
reducing reaction is taking place at the cathode, produc-
ing hydroxyl ions. Thus, the cathodic surface is covered
with a thin layer of hydroxyl ions, giving it a pH much
greater than that of the body of the water. It has been
calculated that this film has a thickness in the 300 to 500
nanometer (1 nanometer = 10–9 meters) range along with
a pH of approximately 11.0 to 11.5 when the bulk water
pH is 8.0.
Consider natural water circulating through the system.
Natural waters contain calcium ions plus bicarbonate and/

Figure 6: Unprotected surface corrodes uniformly.
Figure 7: With 90% of the surface protected, the 10% unprotected
corrodes at 10 times the average rate.

M ay 19 9 9
Water Treatment
or carbonate ions. Even though calcium carbonate is con-
sidered an inefficient cathodic inhibitor, it is well suited to
demonstrate the mechanism of the cathodic inhibition re-
action. Calcium and carbonate ions in the water migrate
into the high pH film, precipitate, and form a layer of cal-
cium carbonate thick enough to effectively stop the elec-
tron transfer (or current flow) but thin enough that it does
not decrease heat transfer. For this process to be success-
ful, any particle of calcium carbonate that extends beyond
the high pH zone must redissolve.
Perhaps the best cathodic inhibitor is polyphosphate.
It reacts in the high pH zone much the same as does
carbonate, forming a calcium polyphosphate film. The
difficulty is that some polyphosphate reverts to ortho-
phosphate, which also reacts with calcium, in the high
pH zone, forming scale rather than a film. For this rea-
son, it is necessary to keep the pH or the phosphate
concentration quite low (pH = 6.2 to 6.7 with 30 to 50
ppm PO4
–3).
In the last few years, a series of calcium phosphate
stabilizers, such as the hydroxylated acrylate polymers,
has been developed. These permit higher concentrations
of calcium and phosphate ions to be carried in the circu-
lating water, again increasing the probability that they
will migrate into the high pH zone.
Film Formation versus Film Maintenance
All corrosion inhibitors require a protective film to
form initially and then to maintain this film with the in-
hibitors (see Table 2). If this is not done properly, the
initial levels of inhibitor will not establish good corro-
sion control. Actually, levels that are too low could ac-
celerate corrosion.
When various inhibitors are used, some establish pro-
tection in a short time even at low levels. Others require
higher levels for longer periods. Chromate takes two to
three days, phosphates three to five days, zinc five to seven
days, and polysilicate and molybdate up to two weeks.
Changes in Technology
The last several years have seen major changes in both
water treatment technology and water system design/
operation.
New galvanized cooling towers are seeing faster form-
ing and more extensive corrosion of the galvanized coat-
ing (white rust) compared to older galvanized towers. A
continuing argument exists between galvanized tower
manufacturers and water treatment suppliers as to who
is to blame. Each has some responsibility. The water treat-
ment can be a contributing factor, while some tower
manufacturers use mill galvanized sheet, which has a thin-
ner coating and uses a different alloy than the older hot
dip galvanizing process. Even the passivation of the gal-
vanizing has changed to a less effective process. Yet if
new galvanized cooling towers are properly passivated
upon commissioning and the water treatment program
incorporates the proper inhibitors, white rust can be
minimized or even prevented.
Chillers are becoming more efficient through the use
of better heat transfer between the cooling water and
the refrigerant. Internally smooth copper tubes have for
years been standard condenser design for cooling water.
The refrigerant has seen externally finned copper tubes
for many years. Today, new chillers often have copper

water systems.

Table 2: Concentration of cooling water corrosion inhibitors
required for film formation and film maintenance.
Table 3: Recent cooling water treatment changes.
condenser tubes that are “rifled” internally, similar to rifle
barrels. They are referred to as “enhanced” tubes and
increase heat transfer from the cooling water.
There are even “super enhanced tubes” that have low
profile fins on the internal surfaces. These new enhanced
copper tubes are only about half as thick as smooth tubes
unless thicker tubes are specially ordered. The new tubes
have greater stressed areas that can result in increased
corrosion and early perforation. Deposits can accumu-
late in the grooves resulting in “fouling” that reduces
heat transfer and can lead to under deposit corrosion.
Conscientious water treatment can minimize these prob-
lems but cannot prevent failure or loss in heat transfer.
The balance between increased chiller efficiency and com-
plex water treatment presents a design dilemma and a
challenge for operators.
Besides changes in materials of construction, water
treatment programs have changed. Table 3 compares
where we were 10 to 15 years ago to where we are now
with conventional cooling water treatment programs.
Chromate is an outstanding corrosion inhibitor for mild
steel, copper alloys, galvanized steel, and aluminum. It
gives many years of equipment life and is rapid to
reprotect if protection is lost. It is forgiving in that it
recovers quickly from upset conditions. It not only is not
food (nutrient) for microorganisms, it is somewhat toxic
to them. It works over a wide pH range of 6.0 to 10.0,
does not form scale or deposits, and colors the water
yellow, so we can detect its presence by just looking at
the water. It was almost a perfect water corrosion inhibi-
tor. However, the EPA considers it toxic, a potential car-
cinogen, and a potential air and water pollutant. The
EPA banned chromate for HVAC cooling water systems in
1995 and in industrial systems several years later.
With chromates no longer available, the water treat-
ment industry turned to a variety of blends of corrosion
inhibitors, first zinc salts, then molybdate salts, and most
commonly now phosphate-based chemicals for mild steel
corrosion control often along with nitrogen-based chemi-
cals for copper alloy corrosion control. Phosphate and
nitrogen chemicals are nutrients for microorganisms, al-
gae, weeds, and grasses (they are fertilizer). Not only do
these chemicals provide less corrosion protection than
chromates, they also provide food for microbiological
organisms.
At the same time, to improve corrosion protection of
cooling system metals, the pH was raised from less than
7.5, with chromate-based corrosion inhibitors to pH lev-
els above 7.5 often up to 8.5 to 9.5. This change had
both good and bad effects. The good effect is that it re-
duces or eliminates the need to use acid to keep pH
levels down. Increased safety of personnel and reduced
damage due to overfeed were welcome changes.
However, higher pH levels have a number of disad-
vantages:
• Higher pH (particularly above 9.0) increases copper
and galvanized steel corrosion.
• Higher pH (particularly with hard water and with
phosphate treatment) requires high levels of scale
inhibitor with resulting substantially increased costs
for cooling water treatment.
• Higher pH reduces the effectiveness of many bio-
cides, including chlorine, thus restricting the avail-
ability of effective biocides, usually increasing bio-
control costs, and making biological control more
difficult.
• Some scale inhibitors used when the pH is higher
are corrosive to copper alloys, galvanized steel, and
even mild steel, particularly if overfed. These scale
inhibitors also could contribute to scale, so if a little
works, a lot may be harmful.
Conclusion
Understanding both corrosion mechanisms and new
technologies of HVAC cooling system design and water
treatment are essential to maintaining efficient opera-
tion and protecting all components in the system. Engi-
neers and operators should not assume but should find
out what new equipment and technologies may exist and
what action is necessary to provide sound operation. New
water treatment technologies may be necessary, but us-
ers should obtain valid verification of their performance
before using them and then monitor the results. If re-
sults are not as expected, make changes before your sys-
tem deteriorates. 

Who Should Be Responsible for Legionella
By Jay S. Gregory
or the last 20 years, the country has become
acutely aware of a new environmental risk
known as Legionella (also known as
Legionnaire’s disease). This article explores
that issue from a legal perspective and consid-
ers potential theories of liability, risk, and de-
fenses to legal claims arising from this disease.\*
provided a vehicle for Legionella to incubate to danger-
ous levels and significantly increase the risk of infection.
Outbreaks of Legionnaire’s disease in office buildings,
hospitals, cruise ships, and hotels continue to generate
media attention and public concern.
In medical parlance, one who suffers from Legion-
naire’s disease is subject to Legionellosis. Legionellosis
manifests in two distinct clinical forms—Legionnaire’s
disease (the more serious of the two) and Pontiac fever.
Legionnaire’s disease is marked by a serious pneumonia
that has particularly debilitating effects on high risk popu-
lations such as the elderly, smokers, and those with
suppressed immune systems.
The disease is transmitted when individuals breathe
in or otherwise aspirate microscopic Legionellae bacte-
ria into the lungs. The incubation period for Legionnaire’s
disease is approximately two to 10 days. If conditions are
favorable, the bacteria multiply exponentially and over-
come the host. Fatality rates for outbreaks of Legionnaire’s
disease are approximately 15%. If caught quickly enough,
treatment with antibiotics is generally effective.
Pontiac fever, on the other hand, usually manifests as
a bad flu with symptoms lasting for a couple of days. The
recovery rate for individuals suffering from Pontiac fever
is essentially 100%.
Some experts suspect the incidence of Legionnaire’s
disease is more widespread than statistics indicate. Since
Legionnaire’s disease is relatively new, not all incidents
of infection are correctly labeled as such. Some scien-
tists estimate that from 2% to 15% of all community-ac-
quired pneumonias that require hospitalization should
be attributed to Legionellae.2 If these percentages are
accurate, tens of thousands of incidents of Legionnaire’s
disease occur annually.
Where is it?
Legionellae exist naturally throughout the environ-
ment, especially in water. Legionellae exist in numerous
lakes and streams in the natural environment, as well as
in taps, cooling towers, and other artificial sources in
Jay S. Gregory is a partner in the law firm of Burns &
Levinson LLP, Boston. Gregory’s practice involves repre-
senting architects, engineers, and other professionals in
construction and related cases.
F
What is Legionnaire’s Disease?
In 1976, the world was introduced to Legionnaire’s Dis-
ease as a result of an American Legion convention at the
Bellevue Stratford Hotel in Philadelphia. Two hundred
twenty-one individuals of the 4,400 attendees became ill,
and 29 died shortly after the convention. This outbreak
garnered massive media attention and spawned fear of an
uncontrollable plague. Within five months of the inci-
dent, investigators from the Centers for Disease Control
issued a comprehensive report linking the newly coined
Legionnaire’s disease to a bacteria known as Legionellae.1
Other experts from numerous disciplines investigated
this incident with hopes of determining its cause, cure,
and preventive mechanisms to avert similar disasters. The
general consensus was that the disease spread via the
HVAC system.
In 1980, a presidential ad hoc committee was appointed
to evaluate Legionnaire’s disease. ASHRAE also has been
involved in the study of this phenomenon and has pub-
lished a comprehensive position paper on Legionnaire’s
disease. The ASHRAE Board of Directors approved the
most recent version on June 25, 1998. We now have a
much better understanding of how Legionnaire’s dis-
ease operates. With this understanding, however, comes
the potential for greater legal liability, especially for those
in the mechanical design and construction professions.
Scientists have determined that the Legionellae bacte-
ria is extremely common, widespread, and has existed
for a long time. In its native habitat, the bacteria is not
particularly dangerous. Legionellae pose an increased
risk to human health only when the bacteria is amplified
and its numbers grow significantly.
One of the ironies of this issue is that highly efficient
heating, ventilating, and air-conditioning systems have
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, May 1999. © Copyright 1999 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper

Practical Guide
M ay 19 9 9
P R AC T I C A L G U I D E
6 3
man-made environments. The mere fact that Legionellae
exist does not necessarily mean there will be an out-
break of Legionnaire’s disease. The most likely cause of
outbreaks involves the action of certain facilitators or am-
plifiers that serve as catalysts to Legionellae growth. Man-
made structures such as cooling towers and evaporative
condensers have been found to act as particularly able
amplifiers. Humidifiers, potable water heaters and hold-
ing tanks, shower heads, faucet aerators, and whirlpool
baths have also been found to act as amplifiers.
Scientists have determined that some Legionellae can
survive routine water treatment used to produce potable
water and then infect entire building systems. Legionellae
can be transported in treated drinking water and find
optimal conditions to colonize plumbing fixtures, espe-
cially in domestic hot water systems. Although scientists
have not been able to determine specifically the concen-
tration (or dose) of Legionellae required to trigger an
epidemic, they generally agree that the higher the con-
centration and virulence of the bacteria, the greater the
likelihood of infection.
At present, there is some debate in the scientific com-
munity whether aerosolized Legionellae or potable water
systems are primarily responsible for the spread of the
disease. This article will not explore this debate. From the
legal perspective, one should assume that both methods
of Legionellae amplification and distribution are likely.
Given the likely mechanisms for amplifying
Legionellae, the prescribed methods for preventing the
transmission of Legionellae focus on the man-made am-
plifier. (This approach makes sense from a logistical and
practical point of view. It would be virtually impossible
to eliminate Legionellae from the natural environment.)
It is common sense that if Legionellae are prevented from
multiplying or amplifying in a specific device or system,
the risk of having sufficient concentrations to effect in-
fection is significantly decreased. The ASHRAE position
paper identifies four strategies for decreasing the poten-
tial for colonization and amplification:
1. Avoid piping that is capped and has no flow (dead
legs). If such piping exists after renovation, it should be
removed from domestic and cooling water systems.
2. Control domestic water temperature to avoid tem-
perature ranges where Legionellae grow. Keep domestic
cold water below 25°C (77°F) and hot water above 55°C
(131°F). Measures to prevent scalding are essential.
3. Apply biocides in accordance with label dosages to
control growth of other bacteria, algae, and protozoa that
may contribute to the nutritional needs of Legionellae.
4. Limit available niches for microbiota. Managing ex-
isting microbiota can be addressed partially by maintain-
ing clean systems, thus achieving this goal. Removing or
preventing sediment accumulation in cooling basins,
decorative fountains, or hot water tanks is an example of
a method to minimize microbial niches.
Besides those four strategies, the position paper
includes specific design recommendations:
1. Design cooling tower and building air intake place-
ments so that air discharged from the cooling tower or
evaporative condenser is not brought directly into the
building air intake.
2. Maintain effective drift eliminators on cooling tow-
ers and evaporative condensers.
3. Ensure air filters for outside air are dry. Water drop-
lets or humidity that condenses on filters provide an
environment where microbes can grow and could be
disbursed to the conditioned space. Air filters should be
cleaned or replaced as directed by the manufacturer.
The ASHRAE position paper also stresses the impor-
tance of preventive maintenance and monitoring. ASHRAE
cautions that “periodic or routine monitoring is never
an effective program if done in the absence of a mainte-
nance program. MORE EMPHASIS SHOULD BE PLACED
ON CLEAN EQUIPMENT AND EXCELLENT REPAIR THAN
ON PERIODIC TESTING WITH CONCURRENT SYSTEM
NEGLECT.”
As the ASHRAE recommendations show, eliminating
and/or preventing Legionnaire’s disease requires effort
during and after construction. This reality increases and
broadens the potential exposure for designers, contrac-
tors, owners, and suppliers of HVAC systems. Not only
will these likely defendants be subject to lawsuits as a
result of the design and installation of HVAC systems,
they will also be subject to lawsuits as a result of the
maintenance and monitoring of the systems.
Claims for Legionnaire’s Disease
As is often the case in our society, shortly after a health
condition or risk is identified, lawyers enter the picture
and develop claims attributing responsibility to all indi-
viduals or entities who arguably might be responsible
for the condition leading to a plaintiff’s injuries. As more
is known about the causes of Legionnaire’s disease, the
more focused lawyers will be in pursuing claims against
purportedly responsible parties.
In particular, as it has become apparent during the
last 20 years that HVAC systems can spread Legionellae,
the individuals and entities “responsible” for such sys-
tems will be subject to greater scrutiny and exposure for
incidents of Legionnaire’s disease. This is not to sug-
gest that such a development is unfair or inappropriate.
People involved in the design, construction, supply, and
maintenance of HVAC systems should keep abreast of
technical developments and tailor their services accord-
ingly. On the other hand, it is prudent for these same
potential defendants to act to minimize their exposure
for lawsuits.
If there were a Legionnaire’s disease outbreak in a
hotel today (like the original outbreak in Philadelphia
in 1976), it is easy to predict who the likely defendants
would be. Depending on who was infected (i.e., a guest
of the hotel, a worker, or some other individual), the
likely defendants would include the HVAC system de-
signers, the hotel owner, the contractors who installed
Water Treatment
6 4
P R AC T I C A L G U I D E
M ay 19 9 9
the system, and the individuals/enti-
ties responsible for maintaining the
system. A typical case involving a
Legionnaire’s disease outbreak would
most likely involve an HVAC system
that complied with applicable stan-
dards and did not run afoul of the
recommendations set forth in the
ASHRAE position paper.
The mere fact that Legionellae in-
fect an HVAC system and an outbreak
occurs does not mean either that the
designer, installer, supplier, or owner
were negligent or that the disease is
attributable to some fault on their part.
Simply put, accidents that are not the
fault of any specific individuals some-
times occur. It is also true that the mere
(Circle No. 31 on Reader Service Card)
fact that the design professional, in-
staller, supplier, and owner might not
be negligent will not dissuade a plain-
tiff from filing suit and attempting to
recover full damages. Next, the nature
of these claims and possible strategies
for combating them will be explored.
Claims Against Designers
An individual infected by Legion-
naire’s disease who sues a design
professional would most likely pur-
sue a claim for negligence in the de-
sign of the HVAC system. In simplest
terms, negligence constitutes a
person’s failure to comply with a duty
owed to others. Generally speaking,
design professionals owe a duty to
their clients to comply with a stan-
dard of care of reasonable practitio-
ners. Designers are not held to a stan-
dard of perfection and are not auto-
matically liable for all errors or omis-
sions in their designs.
Viewed a different way, a design
professional is required to exercise
that level of skill and judgment that
reasonably can be expected of rea-
sonably well qualified designers of
the same background and experience,
under the same or similar circum-
stances as those encountered in the
subject project. It is understood that
reasonably well qualified designers
will undoubtedly make mistakes. As
a consequence, design professionals
are not automatically liable for every
error or omission in their services.
To make its case against a design
professional, a plaintiff will attempt to
show that the outbreak in question was
caused by the design professional’s
failure to do what other reasonably
prudent design professionals would
do. In other words, the plaintiff will
attempt to show that the design pro-
fessional deviated from the applicable
standard of care. To achieve this aim,
the plaintiff’s counsel will retain other
design professionals to pore over the
defendant’s design drawings, exam-
ine the as-built condition, and other-
wise investigate the claim to determine
the extent to which the design pro-
fessional may have failed to comply
with appropriate practice.
Since every design project is
Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.
Practical Guide
M ay 19 9 9
P R AC T I C A L G U I D E
6 5
unique, there is no simple, black and white test to de-
termine whether a design professional has met with the
standard of care. Investigating experts will exercise their
own judgment in determining whether or not the de-
signer was negligent in that particular instance. It bears
noting that experts retained by plaintiff’s counsel face a
subtle yet inherent financial incentive to find fault with
the design professional defendant.
Just as our overall knowledge and understanding of
Legionnaire’s disease developed over time, the standard
of care for designing HVAC systems has evolved to ad-
dress that increased understanding. This evolution means
that what is considered an appropriate design today might
be discovered in the future to have significant adverse
impacts on the amplification or spread of disease. To
minimize exposure for personal injury lawsuits, a de-
sign professional should keep current with developments
in technology and design. A good starting point in this
regard would be professional publications or treatises
such as the ASHRAE position paper on Legionellosis.
As noted earlier, ASHRAE has issued specific recom-
mendations for the design and maintenance of HVAC
systems. To the extent a design professional does not
adhere to these recommendations, a plaintiff’s expert
will have ready ammunition to assert that the designer
failed to meet the standard of care and, therefore, was
negligent. For example, if an engineer designed a sys-
tem with dead legs (or if the engineer left dead legs
intact after a renovation), the plaintiff’s expert will use
this fact as evidence of design negligence.
In a similar vein, the plaintiff’s expert will investigate
whether the design professional left sufficient distance
between the building air intake and the cooling towers
or evaporative condensers. Of course, what constitutes
“sufficient distance” is subject to disagreement and will
be determined by the particular attributes and conditions
of each project site.
Even if the plaintiff’s expert can show that an engi-
neer was negligent in designing the HVAC system, the
plaintiff does not always recover. In addition to estab-
lishing negligence, the plaintiff must establish that this
negligence caused the plaintiff’s injury. In the example
of a design that creates or leaves a dead leg in a water
distribution system, the plaintiff would have to show that
this dead leg was the breeding ground for Legionella
and that bacteria from this particular breeding ground
infected the plaintiff.
Similarly, in the example of the cooling tower, even if
the plaintiff’s expert shows there was insufficient dis-
tance between the cooling tower and an air intake, in
order to recover, the plaintiff would have to show that:
(1) Legionella were amplified in the cooling tower; (2)
aerosolized Legionella were disbursed from the cooling
and entered through the air intake; and (3) these same
Legionella infected the plaintiff. One can imagine that it
is sometimes more difficult for a plaintiff to establish cau-
sation than it is to establish negligence.
Since the ASHRAE position paper includes recommen-
dations for maintenance and chemical treatment of HVAC
systems, claimants will also examine whether the designer
presented adequate recommendations for treatment and
monitoring. To the extent an engineer fails to provide
adequate instructions and/or provides instructions that
are contrary to the ASHRAE position paper, the engineer
opens up exposure for lawsuits. This situation repre-
sents a trap for the unwary on a couple of fronts. Often a
designer has no responsibility or obligation to make rec-
ommendations about maintenance or monitoring re-
gimes. Such would be especially true in situations where
the owner is sophisticated and/or separately retains its
own monitoring and maintenance staff.
Nevertheless, given what is known of the life cycle
and transmission of Legionellae, personal injury plain-
tiffs and owners will likely pursue claims under a “fail-
ure to warn” theory. A prudent design professional, there-
fore, should be careful to avoid situations where it can
be said that the design professional had an obligation
but failed to provide adequate maintenance and moni-
toring instructions or recommendations. To the extent a
design professional has no obligation to make such rec-
ommendations, it would be both appropriate and useful
for the design professional’s contract (or other contem-
poraneous documentation) to state so in explicit terms.
As noted previously, an injured plaintiff would likely
bring claims against multiple parties, including the engi-
neer and the engineer’s client. Once a lawsuit has been
filed, it is not unusual for the client/owner to take a self-
serving position that the engineer was negligent for fail-
ing to warn the owner about appropriate maintenance
or monitoring strategies.
Because the ASHRAE position paper includes explicit
recommendations concerning maintenance and moni-
toring of HVAC systems, plaintiffs’ counsel have ammu-
nition that they otherwise would not have against project
participants who “fail” to provide recommendations. It
might not be enough for design professional defendants
to claim they made no recommendations on the ground
that it was not an explicit part of their contract. The
plaintiff’s expert could easily testify that the standards in
the profession are such that in addition to performing
the explicit requirements set forth in the design
professional’s contract, they require prudent design pro-
fessionals to alert clients to the dangers lurking in poorly
maintained or monitored systems.
After the plaintiff’s expert offers this testimony, the
owner will most likely offer the self-serving testimony
that: (1) the owner had no idea of the ramifications of
failing to maintain or monitor the HVAC system for
Legionellae; (2) the owner is not an expert and relied
entirely on the design professional; and (3) had the de-
sign professional said even one word about the wisdom
of maintaining and monitoring the HVAC system, the
owner would have spared no expense in making sure
that it implemented an appropriate maintenance and
Water Treatment
6 6
P R AC T I C A L G U I D E
M ay 19 9 9
monitoring regime.
To the extent an engineer is obligated by contract or
by conduct to make recommendations for maintenance
and monitoring, the engineer should document those
recommendations in writing. The engineer should also
be sure the recommendations are consistent with cur-
rent thinking on the subject (such as the ASHRAE posi-
tion paper) and are clear. As noted earlier, to the extent
a design professional is not contractually obligated to
offer advice concerning maintenance or monitoring, that
fact should be documented.
It may be helpful to point out that case law in most
jurisdictions does not impose a duty on design profes-
sionals to inform their clients of every known risk that
may attach to a particular system or design. In this re-
gard, absent a duty to speak, failing to disclose a risk
does not constitute fraud or negligence.3
Moreover, as of the time of this writing, the author
has found no cases where a design professional was li-
able for failure to warn of potential damages from an
outbreak of Legionnaire’s disease. From a practical point
of view, this means that a design professional facing a
“failure to warn” claim eventually may prevail, assuming
the court determines the design professional had no af-
firmative duty to speak. Unfortunately, that determina-
tion might come only after literally years of litigation
and tens of thousand of dollars in litigation expenses.
Besides an injured plaintiff, the client is also a likely
claimant against the design professional. Like the other
parties, a client could bring a claim under a negligence
theory. A client also could bring a claim on a breach of
contract theory.
From a practical perspective, this does not significantly
impact the owner’s likelihood of recovery. However, it
might determine how the claim is adjudicated or affect
statutes of limitation, limitation of liability clauses, etc.
An injured plaintiff conceivably could bring a claim
against an owner, but not the design professional. In
that case, the owner would likely bring a third-party claim
against the design professional as a defensive maneuver.
In such a situation, the owner—similar to the injured
individual discussed earlier—would attempt to show that
the design professional failed to comply with the appli-
cable standard of care and was otherwise negligent in
the design or construction administration of the system.
Also as noted before, the owner may attempt to argue
that the design professional failed to provide adequate
warnings or instructions as to the maintenance and/or
monitoring of the system.
Claims Against Contractors
Similar to a claim brought against a design professional,
an injured plaintiff would most likely pursue a claim
against a contractor on a negligence theory. The plaintiff
would attempt to show that the contractor either devi-
ated from the plans or specifications or otherwise failed
to deliver what the design documents require. A good
defense for a contractor is that he or she built exactly
what was shown in the design documents and did not
second guess the designer’s professional judgment. In
other words, a contractor is not responsible for an alleg-
edly defective design that was prepared by someone else.
A contractor might also argue that the injured indi-
vidual was not a third-party beneficiary of its contract
with the owner. Simply stated, this argument would be
that the contractor owed a duty to its client by contract
but not to unrelated third parties. In many jurisdictions,
this argument will not prevail if the plaintiff can show
that a likely outcome of the contractor’s negligence was
a foreseeable risk of harm to a third party such as the
injured individual. For example, if the plaintiff can es-
tablish that a likely result of the contractor’s defective
construction was the creation of an “amplifier” that could
lead to an outbreak of Legionnaire’s disease, the con-
tractor would be hard pressed to succeed with a third-
party beneficiary defense.
Just as design professionals can be the target of a claim
based on failure to warn, so can contractors. This expo-
sure increases to the extent the contractor assumes re-
sponsibility for the engineering or specification of the
HVAC system. Accordingly, a prudent contractor should
either make clear that the owner (or some other party) is
responsible for determining and implementing the main-
tenance and monitoring program or should provide clear,
written, and up-to-date maintenance and monitoring rec-
ommendations.
Claims Against Manufacturer or Supplier
To a large degree, equipment manufacturers and in-
stallers stand in the same position as contractors. There-
fore, they have similar exposure and defenses. In addi-
tion to being subject to negligence claims, however, sup-
pliers and manufacturers would be subject to a different
breed of claim arising from the fact that they provided a
product that allegedly caused harm. This past century
has seen a dramatic increase in claims known as product
liability suits. In general terms, product liability claim-
ants argue that the manufacturer put an unreasonably
dangerous product into the chain of commerce and that
the product harmed the plaintiff.
Plaintiffs often argue that a product is unreasonably
dangerous on its own. Chain saws, lawn mowers, and
snow blowers are examples of such products. Plaintiffs
also argue that even if an individual part or product is
not dangerous on its own, it became dangerous as a re-
sult of the way it was combined with other aspects of the
overall system. For example, a supplier or manufacturer
of pipe to be used in a hot water distribution system can
reasonably assume that the pipe, on its own, is fairly be-
nign. If this pipe, however, becomes a “dead leg” in the
new system, the plaintiff might argue that it has become
unreasonably dangerous. Of course, the logical defense
to such a claim is that the manufacturer and supplier did
not design the system and, therefore, should not be held
Practical Guide
M ay 19 9 9
P R AC T I C A L G U I D E
6 7
responsible for this dead leg. “All we
did was supply or manufacture the
pipe,” they will claim. Such a defense,
while strong, is not fail safe.
It is not unusual in construction
projects for suppliers or manufactur-
ers to review design drawings to de-
termine quantities of required mate-
rials. A resourceful plaintiff’s attor-
ney will argue that the supplier or
manufacturer should have noted in
reviewing the plans that the design
included dead legs contrary to
ASHRAE recommendations. The
plaintiff’s attorney will argue that the
manufacturer or supplier was negli-
gent to sit idly by, allowing its prod-
uct to be used in this obviously dan-
gerous system. This argument will
have more merit to the extent the
supplier or manufacturer provided
specialized equipment or materials
and/or assumed a greater role in the
specification of the HVAC system.
In addition to a negligence theory,
the plaintiff will also pursue a failure
to warn theory against the manufac-
turer or supplier. For example, a
plaintiff might argue that the manu-
facturer of a cooling tower or evapo-
rative condenser failed to provide ad-
equate warning or instruction on
how to minimize or eliminate drift
or aerosolization. Similarly, a plain-
tiff might argue that the manufacturer
failed to provide adequate warning
and instructions for maintenance or
monitoring. As noted earlier for the
design professional and the contrac-
tor, a prudent manufacturer or sup-
plier should put in writing the ex-
tent to which it is not providing in-
structions or recommendations.
Claims Against Owners
In the example of a guest at a hotel
contracting Legionnaire’s disease as
a result of the amplification of
Legionellae through the hotel’s HVAC
system, the most likely claim will be
that the owner was negligent in ei-
ther maintaining or monitoring the
HVAC system. A court likely would
require the plaintiff to demonstrate
that the owner failed to observe a
duty of care that the owner owed the
injured party. In most jurisdictions,
owners of hotels and similar institu-
tions are held to an extremely high
standard for the care and safety of
their guests.
To minimize the risk of liability, a
prudent owner should comply with
industry recommendations such as
those in the ASHRAE position paper
or similar authorities. The plaintiff’s
counsel will examine the owner’s
maintenance and monitoring prac-
tices in great detail and will capital-
ize on any deviation from recom-
mended practice. Just as the
plaintiff’s counsel will likely retain
an engineering expert to support the
Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.
Water Treatment
6 8
P R AC T I C A L G U I D E
M ay 19 9 9
claim against the design professional,
the plaintiff’s counsel is also likely
to retain a specialized hotel expert
who would (if supported by the evi-
dence) provide testimony that the
owner’s maintenance and monitor-
ing regime was inadequate for par-
ticular reasons. To combat such a
claim, a prudent owner should have
clear documentation of what exactly
it did, when, how often, and why.
Conclusion
As seen earlier, developments in
our understanding of the cause and
prevention of Legionnaire’s disease
provide an opportunity for plaintiffs
to develop potential legal theories
against various participants in con-
struction projects. In other words,
knowledge and understanding pave
the way for attributing blame and as-
signing legal liability. As in most as-
pects of construction, a prudent de-
signer, contractor, or supplier must
keep abreast of technological devel-
opments. One can argue that the bur-
den of being a professional demands
that one keep informed of develop-
ments and the state of the art in his
or her profession. Such dedication
to being informed will pay dividends
in terms of minimizing risk.
One can similarly decrease risk of
legal claims by careful contract draft-
ing. To avoid liability under a failure
to warn theory, one should document
in writing: (1) that one has no respon-
sibility to provide recommendations or
instructions as to maintenance or moni-
toring of the completed system; or (2)
if one is responsible for providing such
instruction, then such instruction
should be clear, easy to follow, and
consistent with prevailing standards.
Even the most prudent designer or
contractor cannot avoid all risk of
claims. Lawsuits often will be brought
in situations where some or all the
defendants were entirely prudent and
without objective fault. To fight such
a lawsuit effectively, one must know
the risks going in and be prepared to
minimize those risks using strategies
like those outlined previously.
Note
\*This article does not focus on the related
and more extensive issue of sick building
syndrome and/or multiple chemical
sensitivity.
References
1. Sharrar, R.G., “Follow up - Respiratory
Disease - Philadelphia,” Morbidity Mor-
tality Report,25(1976):270, 275–276.
2. Stout, J.E., et al., “Potable water as a
cause of sporadic cases of community-
acquired legionnaire’s disease,” The New
England Journal of Medicine 326(3).
3. See e.g., Nei v. Boston Survey Consult-
ants, Inc., 388 Mass. 320 (1983). 

ASHRAE JOURNAL
About the Author
Jonathan T

Jonathan T. Crane
. Crane
. Crane
. Crane
. Crane, AIA, is an architect at HOK
Architects in Atlanta, Ga., specializing in laboratory
facilities. He is a member of TC 9.10, Laboratory
Systems, and participated in the recent updates to
the CDC/NIH Guidelines.
he information presented here is important to the HVAC&R
industry due to the increasing number of biological containment
facilities being developed or planned in the U.S. and abroad. The
popular press has taken outbreaks of emerging diseases and the threat of
bio-terrorism and has created misleading impressions of the hazards in-
volved in these laboratories. A dialog that takes a rational look at the engi-
neering needs of these laboratories should be undertaken to allow them to
be designed in a manner commensurate with the actual risk.
T
Introduction
Author Richard Preston popularized
the term “hot zone” as a metaphor for labo-
ratories designed to provide high levels
of biological containment. Generally,
these are laboratories designed at
Biosafety Level 4 (BSL4) as defined in
the CDC/NIH Guideline “Biosafety in
Microbiological and Biomedical Labora-
tories.”1 This article, however, includes
discussions of the design issues encoun-
tered at Biosafety Level 3 (BSL3) as well
as BSL4 as it reaches a broader audience.
It is intended to be a broad discussion of
the issues involved in these labs, not a
technical guide technical information is
better found at the source. The reasons
for developing laboratories at these levels
of containment are to protect the user and
protect the environment while providing
proper conditions to conduct research.
Hot Zone Laboratories
Biosafety Level 3 and 4 laboratories are
ratory facility requirements with agent
summary statements that categorize
various infectious agents.
Much of the knowledge used to de-
velop the high-level containment require-
ments was carried over from the biologi-
cal weapons programs of the 50s and 60s,
where much research was done by the
Army and Navy on aerosol generation
and containment of infectious agents. As
virologist Karl Johnson said at the 5th
National Conference on Biosafety:
“The level 4 portion of our current
guidelines were assembled under high
pressure in 1976, principally by active
and former biosafety professionals at Ft.
Detrick. The world of their experience was
one of gallons of organisms, and of large
volume high concentration intentionally
created aerosols. Anthrax and Coxiella
Burnetii loomed large in this work, and
both were known to be extremely resis-
tant to environmental degradation. …
“The implied conclusion from the
BSL4 standards so written is that such
agents are of great hazard, not only to
the laboratorian, but to the community.
… When, in fact it is simply not so for
most, if not all, of the viruses on the list.
They are principally membrane-bound
RNA agents, which are quite fragile un-
less wrapped in heavy overcoats of ex-
traneous protein. Millions and millions
of dollars have been spent to build the
liquid waste systems of such virus labo-
ratories, while the original offenders, an-
thrax and Q fever, now reside in the hap-
pier Elysian fields of BSL3 where such
effluent treatment is not required.”2
Hot Zone Laboratories
Jonathan T. Crane, AIA
Associate Member ASHRAE
developed to allow work with infectious
agents that have serious consequences of
disease. In addition, these laboratories may
be used for environmental protection from
agents hazardous to animals or from crops
that may pose little consequence for hu-
mans but could result in serious economic
problems if released into the environment.
Containment of these agents is accom-
plished by using primary barriers such as
Class II or Class III biological safety cabi-
nets and secondary barriers that are sur-
rounding facilities and supporting engi-
neering systems.
The formal guidelines defining facil-
ity requirements are relatively recent.
Their development was the result of the
Recombinant Advisory Committee
(RAC), which started in the late 1970s
to define containment requirements for
the emerging field of recombinant
DNA. These guidelines listed facilities
as P-1 through P-4 (nomenclature still
heard today, although the RAC has
since adopted the CDC/NIH BMBL). In
the early 1980s, this guideline was de-
veloped in a joint effort between CDC
and NIH to bring together laboratory
practices, safety equipment and labo-
Design Commensurate With Risk
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, June 1999. © Copyright 1999 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper
form without permission of ASHRAE.
J u n e 1 9 9 9
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
2 9
L A B O R A T O R I E S
Therefore, a rational approach to risk assessment and sound
engineering is the prudent course in the design of these facilities.
Biocontainment HVAC Design Issues
The mechanical systems for the BSL3 and BSL4 facility play
an important role in safety and are often the most complex parts
of the facility. They control the movement of fluids (including
air) through and out of the facility.
For BSL3 laboratories, a ducted ventilation system creates
directional airflow from “clean” areas into the laboratory toward
“contaminated” areas. BSL3 laboratories require this directional
airflow to assist in the containment of microorganisms in the
event of a spill or improper manipulation of agents. At BSL3, all
work producing infectious aerosols is to be done in a primary
containment device. The normal ambient air in the lab is not
contaminated. Laboratory personnel must verify that the direc-
tion of the airflow is into the laboratory prior to beginning work.
A device that indicates and confirms directional airflow into the
laboratory should be provided.
HEPA filtration of exhaust air is not normally required for
BSL3 laboratories unless the exhaust cannot be dispersed away
from air intakes or other occupied spaces. Certain agents or
specific risk assessment of the work to be accomplished may
warrant the inclusion of HEPA filtration of the exhaust. For
these conditions, additional environmental protection such as
personnel showers and effluent decontamination may be
warranted.
Although not formally referred to in the guidelines, this level
of protection is commonly referred to as BSL3+ or enhanced
BSL3. An example of an agent that requires enhanced BSL3 is
the recent strain of avian flu in Hong Kong. The U.S. Depart-
ment of Agriculture regulations limit the study of this agent to
laboratories with upgraded environmental protection to keep
this zoonotic agent from infecting U.S. poultry flocks.
BMBL describes two models for Biosafety Level 4 laborato-
ries: the “Cabinet Laboratory” where all handling of the agent
is performed in a Class III biological safety cabinet and the
“Suit Laboratory” where personnel wear a protective suit. BSL4
laboratories may be based on either model or a combination of
both models in the same facility. In all BSL4 laboratories, a
dedicated non-recirculating ventilation system is provided. The
air supply and exhaust systems for the BSL4 laboratory must
not serve other areas of the facility and must be balanced to
ensure directional airflow from the area of least hazard to the
area of greatest potential hazard.
In BSL4 cabinet laboratories, the general supply air and
exhaust air from the cabinet room is treated by a passage
through a HEPA filter prior to entry to the room and discharge
to the outside. The exhaust from the Class III cabinet passes
through two HEPA filters in series at the cabinet. In BSL4 Suit
Laboratories, the supply air to the suit area, decontamination
shower and decontamination airlock is HEPA filtered. The gen-
eral room exhaust air from these areas passes through two
HEPA filters in series. In all cases, the air is discharged away
from occupied spaces and air intakes. The HEPA filters should
be located as near as practicle to the source to minimize the
length of potentially contaminated ductwork. The HEPA filter
housings allow for in-place decontamination of the filter prior
to removal, or removal of the filter by a bag-in bag-out method
for subsequent decontamination or destruction. The design of
the HEPA filter housing should facilitate validation of the filter
installation.
Directional airflow created by pressure differentials be-
tween spaces is used to contain potentially contaminated
air in the BSL4 laboratory. The directional airflow and pres-
sure gradients move from areas of lesser hazard to areas of
higher hazard. Most work with specimens occurs in primary
containment devices. The highest hazard areas are animal
holding and necropsy rooms due to aerosols that may be
difficult to fully contain.
The subject of specific pressure differential is often dis-
cussed and debated. No specific differentials are required
by BMBL, and design basis varies widely between labora-
tories. They range from a high of 0.4 in. w.g. (100 Pa) incre-
ments with a 0.8 in. w.g. (200 Pa) differential between the
animal entry corridor and the suit area at the Australian
animal disease center at Geelong3 to a 0.04 in. w.g. (10 Pa)
differential at each step with 0.12 in. w.g. (30 Pa) between
the exterior corridor and the suit area in the new BSL4 facil-
ity in Lyon, France.4 A common differential used is 0.2 in.
Major HVAC Requirements for BSL3 and BSL4
Major HVAC Requirements for BSL3 and BSL4
Major HVAC Requirements for BSL3 and BSL4
Major HVAC Requirements for BSL3 and BSL4
Major HVAC Requirements for BSL3 and BSL4
Biosafety Level 3
 Ducted exhaust ventilation
 Directional airflow from “clean” toward “contaminated”
 Exhaust air is not recirculated outside the BSL3
 Exhaust is discharged to the outside
 Exhaust must not be re-entrained
 Air-gap connections for Biological Safety Cabinets
 Additional environmental protection may be required
Biosafety Level 4—Cabinet Laboratory
 Dedicated non-recirculating ventilation
 Directional airflow from least hazard to greatest hazard
 Differential pressure/directional airflow is alarmed
 Supply and exhaust are interlocked
 General room supply and exhaust is HEPA filtered
 Exhaust must not be re-entrained
 In situ decontamination of HEPA filter housings
Biosafety Level 4—Suit Laboratory
 Dedicated non-recirculating ventilation
 Directional airflow from least hazard to greatest hazard
 Differential pressure/directional airflow is alarmed
 Supply and exhaust are interlocked
 Exhaust must not be re-entrained
 In situ decontamination of HEPA filter housings
 Suit area exhaust has two HEPA filters in series
 Suit area air pressure is lower than that of any
adjacent area
 Suit area supply is HEPA filtered
 Air-gap connections for Biological Safety Cabinets
3 0
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
J u n e 1 9 9 9
w.g. (50 Pa). The basis for determining
the differential should be to create suf-
ficient directional airflow to allow con-
tainment to occur during planned or un-
planned breaches in the barrier; how-
ever, the lower the differential pressure,
the easier it will be to move in and out
of the facility.
Integration of Primary
Containment
Biological safety cabinets are required
at both BSL3 and BSL4. They have been
shown to be the most effective facility
component in reducing laboratory-ac-
quired infections.5 At BSL3 and in BSL4
suit laboratories, the most common type
used are Class II, Type A cabinets that re-
circulate the HEPA filtered air back into
the room. At times, these cabinets are
thimble connected with exhaust located
directly above the cabinet outtake. This
exhausts the cabinet outside the facility
and allows the heat from the cabinets to
be removed directly from the room. If other
than minute quantities of toxins or gases
are used within the cabinets, consideration
should be given to using Class II, Type B2
fully exhausted cabinets; however, this is
normally the exception rather than the rule.
In some BSL3 laboratories and in BSL4
cabinet labs, Class III biological safety
cabinets are used to provide a higher de-
gree of containment. These cabinets have
HEPA filtered supply and double HEPA fil-
tered exhaust with worker access through
sealed gloveports.
Small- to medium-sized animal caging
at BSL3 and BSL4 is generally accom-
plished with ventilated cage racks that
either vent directly into the exhaust sys-
tem or are HEPA filtered and vent back
into the room. In all cases, the biological
safety cabinets and animal caging, if con-
nected to the supply or exhaust system,
must be connected so that system opera-
tion or malfunction does not affect the
proper operation of these primary con-
tainment devices.
Common Problems
Many engineering issues have been
uncovered during the design and
shakeout of these facilities over the years.
Problem issues to consider include: locat-
ing the filtration far away from the lab,
which requires long runs of gas-tight duct
that strains budgets, schedules and
constructability; designing systems that
have too little tolerance to balance airflow;
providing insufficient fan capacity to
maintain directional airflow and pressure
differentials; lack of redundancy; and fail-
ure to recognize the interactions of the
systems serving the laboratory with the
systems serving the rest of the building.
Most of these issues can be avoided if
common sense is applied to the design.
Conclusion
The designer venturing into the “hot
zone” should take care to fully under-
stand the concepts behind the require-
ments of containment of hazards at
Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.
J u n e 1 9 9 9
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
3 1
L A B O R A T O R I E S
BSL3 and BSL4. Then appropriate decisions regarding the
level of engineered systems can be made. Often, the old
axiom of “simpler is better” becomes more appropriate as
one moves upward in the level of containment.
The most common misunderstanding of these laboratories
is the belief that they are designed to provide absolute contain-
ment. The greatest potential for contamination of the outside
environment occurs when the scientist leaves the laboratory.
The only method to ensure absolute containment would be to
never let the scientists out of the lab, a prospect unlikely to
attract many scientists. Rather, the design criteria for these labo-
ratories is to provide a reasonable certainty, commensurate to
the risk that the agent will pose to the community, that the
agents will remain in the lab in the event of unusual circum-
stances or accidents.

2. Johnson, K.M. 1998. “Why do containment?” J.Y. Richmond (ed.),
Proceedings of the 5th National Symposium on Biosafety, p.25-30.
American Biological Safety Association.
3. Abraham, G., et al. 1997. “Handling of experimental animals in-
fected with a risk group 4 virus,” p. 26-35. Journal of the American
Biological Safety Association, 2(4).
4. Fischer-Hoch, S.P., et al. 1998. The Biosafety Level 4 Suit Labora-
tory, Lyon France. In J.Y. Richmond (ed.), Proceedings of the 5th
National Symposium on Biosafety. American Biological Safety Asso-
ciation, p.107-109.
Bibliography

and mechanical implications of biological safety cabinet selection,
location and venting.” Laboratory HVAC Systems: Design, Validation,
Commissioning. ASHRAE.

ASHRAE JOURNAL
odern energy management and control systems have alarm and
trend features that allow building operators to monitor and
troubleshoot building systems. The alarm feature can provide
nearly immediate feedback when, for instance, a sensor value is outside
specified limits. Using the trend feature, building operators can view graphs
of sensor and controller output versus time. In the case of an alarm, infor-
mation obtained using the trend feature can assist the operator in isolating
the cause of the alarm. However, it can be difficult to select the alarm limits.
If the limits are too narrow, the building control system likely will issue a
number of false alarms and building operators eventually will ignore them.
If the limits are too wide, faulty conditions will tend to go unnoticed be-
cause alarms are not issued.
M
into a small number of indices that can be
used to quickly assess the performance
of control loops. Indices also can be com-
puted that allow building operators to
identify actuators and valves that are
wearing out prematurely due to excessive
movement. The algorithm is computation-
ally efficient and requires only limited
memory for archiving data. Thus, it can
reside in local digital controllers.
Control Performance Monitor
Figures 1 and 2 provide an overview
of the function of the control performance
monitor and its implementation. Figure 1
is a block diagram of a digital control sys-
tem that has a control performance moni-
tor. The feedback controller adjusts the
actuator to bring the sensed value for the
process output toward the setpoint. Si-
multaneously, the control performance
monitor collects data and computes indi-
ces that quantify the control system per-
formance and that can be accessed over
the communication trunk.
Figure 2 depicts control performance
monitors residing in digital controllers at
various locations on the communication
trunk. Although the performance indices
could be computed in a central computer,
there are several advantages to perform-
ing these computations in local digital
controllers.
First, network communication speed
makes it difficult to refresh all data points
at the operator workstation at the same
rate at which local digital controllers are
sampling and controlling a particular con-
trol loop (in many cases this can be every
second). Computing the performance in-
dices at the local digital controllers and
downloading values on a hourly or daily
basis alleviates this bottleneck.
Second, by distributing the process-
ing, the amount of processing performed
at the operator workstation is reduced. A
third advantage is that a service person
can connect a portable computer to the
network and read the performance indi-
ces for various controllers. This is impor-
tant for control systems that do not have
an operator workstation on the commu-
nication trunk.
Finally, there are times when the com-
munication network is not connected or
is malfunctioning. After the network com-
munication is re-established, a building
operator could determine quickly the per-
formance of the control system by pull-
ing data from the local digital controllers.
On-line Monitoring
And Fault Detection
John E. Seem, Ph.D.
Member ASHRAE,
John M. House, Ph.D.
Associate Member ASHRAE
and
Richard H. Monroe
Although alarm and trend features pro-
vide significant monitoring and trouble-
shooting capabilities, challenges remain.
The size and complexity of building sys-
tems has led to data overload at the op-
erator workstation. Tools and algorithms
are needed to consolidate the informa-
tion that arrives at the workstation into a
clear picture of the system status.
This article describes one such algo-
rithm for monitoring control performance
and presents laboratory and real building
data from variable-air-volume (VAV) boxes
that demonstrate the utility of the algo-
rithm for isolating faults. This algorithm,
referred to as the control performance
monitor, compresses large volumes of data

This article focuses on two performance indices. The first
provides an estimate of the process error (or simply error),
defined as the setpoint value of the process output minus the
measured value of the process output. This index provides an
indication of how well the controller maintains the process
output at its setpoint value, with values near zero indicating
good control. The second performance index provides a mea-
sure of the average duty cycle of an actuator. A large value of
average duty cycle is an indication of unstable control that
could cause the actuator to wear out prematurely.
The performance index for the absolute value of the error is
determined from

where is the exponentially weighted moving average (EWMA)
of the absolute value of the error at time t, is the EWMA of
the absolute value of the error at time t – T where T is the
sampling time of the digital controller, λ is the exponential
smoothing constant, and et is the error at time t.1 is a measure
of how well the controller has maintained the process output at
the setpoint value over a period of time. This performance index
will be large for both persistent errors and for control systems
where the process output oscillates around the setpoint.
The performance index for duty cycle is determined from

(2)
where is the EWMA of the duty cycle at time t, is the
EWMA of the duty cycle at time t – T, and dt is the duty cycle
between time t and t – T. (The duty cycle at time t is the fraction
of time an electric actuator has been operating between time t
and t – T.) A large value of would indicate that mechanical
components (such as actuators, valves, or dampers) are mov-
ing excessively and could wear out prematurely.
There are a number of additional performance indices that
can be used to assess control performance, including the pro-
cess error, process output, valve and damper control signals,
and the number of starts, stops and reversals of an actuator.
Each provides useful information for both assessing the con-
trol performance and for troubleshooting when a fault is de-
tected.
The main challenge to using Equations 1 and 2 is the selec-
tion of the proper smoothing constant, λ. If λ is too large, the
performance indices will take on large values following a load
disturbance or setpoint change. If λ is too small, the time re-
quired to detect a fault will be excessive. The following guideline
can be used to select a smoothing constant that will give a good
estimate of the control performance:
s

<
< λ
(3)
where ts is the settling time of the control system. The settling
time is the time required for the process output to settle to and
remain within ± δ of the final value. Common values for δ are
1%, 2%, or 5% of the size of the setpoint change.2,3 Note that
the selection of λ depends only on the dynamics of the control
loop and the sampling time of the digital controller. Hence, if a
single control loop is considered (e.g., the flow control loop in
a VAV box controller), Equations 1 and 2 would use the same
value of λ.
Conventional moving averages have a fixed-length-sliding
window of data with each data point contributing equally to the
average. EWMAs weigh recent values more heavily than past
values. The smaller the value of λ, the greater the influence of
past values on the EWMA. When λ = T/(X·ts) where X is an
arbitrary number, approximately 63.2% of the weighting in the
computation of the EWMA is assigned to the data between
times t and t – X·ts.
Another important difference between conventional mov-
ing averages and EWMAs is the amount of data that must be
stored to make the computations. For EWMAs, Equations 1
and 2 show that only the previous value of the EWMA must be

B U I L D I N G A U T O M A T I O N S Y S T E M M O N I T O R I N G
stored. Other terms in these equations are current values. Con-
ventional moving averages based on the past n values of a
variable must store all n values. This storage savings can be-
come important when attempting to implement numerous per-
formance indices in a controller with limited memory.
VAV Laboratory Results
Yoshida described a number of faults that cause pressure-
independent VAV systems to fail.4 Examples include stuck
dampers and actuators, fouling of the reheat coil, improper
installation of the reheat coil valve, incorrect readings from
the flow measurement station, drifting of the zone tempera-
ture sensor, and oscillating VAV dampers caused by poor con-
troller tuning.
Laboratory tests were performed to assess the capability of
the control performance monitor to identify symptoms in VAV
boxes subjected to faults. The laboratory system consisted of a
VAV air-handling unit (AHU) that supplied air to three VAV
boxes. The faults introduced consisted of a stuck damper in
one of the VAV boxes, and an unstable supply fan control loop.
(The supply fan was controlled to maintain the static pressure
in the supply duct at a fixed setpoint value and, therefore, would
affect all VAV boxes served by the AHU.) The stuck damper
fault was detected easily by observing that the EWMA for the
absolute value of the flow error for that particular VAV box
increased as the zone load changed.
Figure 3 shows the supply air pressure during the test for
the unstable static pressure control. Data for the initial two and
one-half hours of the test show that the controller is performing
well and is maintaining the supply air pressure at the setpoint
value of 249 Pa (1 in. of water). The controller gain was then
increased by a factor of 4.5, causing the static pressure to oscil-
late. The oscillations of the static pressure caused the dampers
in the VAV boxes to move excessively.
Figure 4 shows the EWMAs for the duty cycle of the three
VAV boxes. Notice the obvious increase in the EWMAs caused
by the oscillation of the dampers. Hence, this EWMA can be
used to identify actuators that are likely to fail prematurely due
to excessive movement.
AHU Laboratory Results
The AHU system described in the previous section also was
tested to assess the capability of the control performance moni-
tor to detect six other AHU faults. The faults introduced con-
sisted of a complete failure of the supply fan, a complete failure
of the return fan, a stuck cooling coil valve, a complete failure of
the supply air thermocouple, a complete failure of the supply
airflow station, and a complete failure of the return airflow sta-
tion. Plots of the performance indices versus time showed that
the performance monitor successfully identified the presence
of a fault in each case.5
VAV Field Test Results
The utility of the performance indices is best demonstrated
by examining their implementation in a real building applica-
tion. In one particular building, a subcontractor installed new
digital controllers for 24 dual-duct VAV boxes. The subcontrac-
tor and building engineer were confident that the VAV control-
lers were installed correctly and operating properly. The
controlled variables were sampled every 1.5 seconds. The
smoothing constants for the flow error and temperature error
were 0.000833 and 0.000104, respectively. An average duty cycle
was determined from the last time the controller was reset. The
figures that follow represent a snapshot of the operation of the
24 VAV boxes at a particular point in time.
Figure 5 shows the normalized flow error for the 24 VAV
boxes. To normalize the flow error, the EWMA of the absolute
value of the flow error was divided by the design maximum flow
rate for cooling. The normalized flow errors for Boxes 16 and 18
are significantly larger than those for the other boxes. Further
investigation revealed a defective capacitor in the electric mo-
tor for Box 16. Box 18 was found to have an incorrectly installed
electric damper actuator. Specifically, it had been locked to the
damper shaft 90 degrees from the correct position causing the
VAV box to be wide open when the VAV controller issued a
closed command, and to be completely closed when the VAV
Figure 3: Static pressure during laboratory test.
Figure 4: EWMA of the duty cycle during laboratory test.
2 4

controller issued a command for full flow.
For the fault-free VAV boxes (i.e., all boxes except 16 and 18),
the average normalized flow error was 0.047 and the standard
deviation was 0.027. For VAV Boxes 16 and 18, the normalized
flow errors were 0.866 and 1.136, respectively. These flow errors
are 30 and 40 standard deviations larger than the average value
for fault-free boxes.
Figure 6 shows the EWMA for the duty cycle of the 24 VAV
boxes. There appears to be no significant outlier in the duty
cycle data. If the large normalized flow errors seen in Figure 5

VAV boxes.

2 5
B U I L D I N G A U T O M A T I O N
were due to unstable control, then larger than normal duty cycle
values would also be observed for these boxes.
Figure 7 shows the EWMA of the absolute value of the
temperature errors for the 24 VAV controllers. The temperature
errors indicate that four or five VAV boxes could be operating in
the presence of faults (Boxes 3, 4, 10, 16 and 19). Figure 8 is a
plot of the EWMA of the temperature errors versus the EWMA
of the normalized flow errors. This figure can help a building
operator prioritize maintenance for boxes with the high-normal-
ized flow errors by identifying which of the boxes has the high-
est temperature error and, therefore, the highest likelihood of
uncomfortable occupants. Although Box 18 has a larger flow
error than Box 16, Box 16 probably should be examined and
repaired first because of the large temperature error associated
with this box. The controllers for Boxes 3, 4, 10 and 19 should be
monitored further to determine if they require maintenance.
Conclusions
Figure 7: EWMA of the absolute value of the temperature
error for the field-installed VAV boxes.
Figure 8: EWMA of the absolute value of the temperature
error versus EWMA of the normalized flow error for the
field-installed VAV boxes.
Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.
