Chillers
pump curve to meet the system curve. This
offered a potential solution of simply op-
erating fewer pumps than were used be-
fore. This caused the pumps to overload
and unfortunately, the frames could not
accommodate larger electric motors.
If the pump motors were not under-
sized, the needed pump performance
could possibly have been met by allow-
ing the pump(s) to ride down the pump
curve. The lower head/higher flow point
is at a less efficient point on the pump’s
curve, so greater savings can be obtained
by a pump that produces the proper flow
and head at its maximum efficiency point.
In addition, far more net-positive suc-
tion head is required at high flow and low
head of a pump selected for lower flow
and higher head performance. This often
causes pump manufacturers not to show
or guarantee operation at the lower part of
the pump curve.
Another challenge of the existing sys-
tem was the requirement for manual selec-
tion of primary chilled water pumps. The
plant operator had to mix and match pumps
to serve the various combinations of chill-
ers required to meet the load from the cam-
pus and to charge the TES tank. To im-
prove the reliability of the system, most
operators tended to run more pumps than required to provide the
necessary flow.
Another important issue was that the chillers had different
design ∆Ts, different evaporator flow rates, different evaporator
pressure drops, and different minimum allowable evaporator leav-
ing chilled water temperatures. Table 2 shows the chiller evapo-
rator design conditions at the beginning of the retrofit project.
Given these problems and the obvious oversize of the pri-
mary loop chilled water pumps, an opportunity existed to re-
duce energy consumption by reducing the power consumption
of the pumps. While trimming the pump impellers could reduce
the head produced by the pumps at design flows, that solution
would not provide maximum energy savings nor address the
other problems discussed earlier. An ideal solution also would
improve operation and control. Because of these and other
factors, neither the solution of trimming impellers nor operating
fewer pumps was selected.
A variable-primary-flow (VPF) system was not considered
because the secondary distribution pumps were already in-
stalled (therefore, no capital cost savings could be gained) and
because the older central plant chillers (and their controls) could
have trouble with variable evaporator flow.
System Design Issues
Reliability and durability of the pumps was an important is-
sue as there was not enough space to install redundant pumps
for each chiller. The design criteria established a minimum 30-
year life. The following were also important issues:
1. Accomodating the different chiller evaporator parameters
in Table 2.
2. Accomodating for varying campus ∆Ts between summer
and winter periods and still keep the chillers fully loaded.
3. Achieving the minimum chilled water supply temperature
leaving each chiller.
4. Accomodating varying primary loop differential-pressures
and accommodating potential increased primary loop dif-
ferential-pressures as the central plant is expanded.
5. Accomodating varying flow rates through the chillers.
6. Keeping the chilled plant operating in both TES and non-
TES modes during construction.
7. Making the project self-funding from energy savings with
the projected saving resulting in no more than a seven-
year payback.
8. Fitting into the limited space available and the piping con-
straints of the existing plant installation.
The Solution
Based on discussions with UCI staff, the authors determined
that providing a single chilled water pump for each chiller would
provide for operational simplicity and was the most desirable
solution based on the earlier discussion. Pumps on a common
header would need pump heads high enough for the chiller with
the highest pressure drop (approximately 2.7 times the minimum
chiller pressure drop). Spacing and cost constraints only allowed

for a single pump per chiller. UCI Facilities
keeps complete spare rotating-assemblies
in stock so the staff can quickly repair a
pump that fails. The design incorporated
variable frequency drives to maintain a
constant selected flow rate regardless of
changing header differential pressures,
and to accommodate the need for differ-
ent chiller evaporator flow rates at differ-
ent times of the year.
To accommodate as wide a variation in
chilled water return temperatures as pos-
sible and still maintain the minimum allow-
able leaving chilled water temperature, the
retrofit installed an automatic modulating
bypass control valve at each chiller. The
bypass maintains the leaving chilled wa-
ter supply temperature by returning part
of the chilled water supply to the chiller
inlet. For example, if the CWR temperature
to chiller number No. 2 is 55°F (13°C), nor-
mally, at the maximum ∆T, the CWS tem-
perature would be 43°F (6°C). To obtain
the desired 39°F (3.8°C) CWS, some CWS
is recirculated to the chiller inlet for a
blended chiller entering water temperature
of 51°F (10.5°C). Figure 1 provides a sche-
matic diagram of the designed system.
Table 3 provides the design operating conditions of the new
primary chilled water pumps.
Because energy savings were an essential factor behind the
decision to use new pumps, UCI maximized design point pump
and motor efficiencies. UCI specified factory performance test-
ing and individual calibrated pump curves.
UCI selected double-suction horizontal split-case pumps as
the basis for design. With the existing pipe geometry, this style
of pump allowed for the simplest installation with the minimum
number of elbows.
Automatic two-position control valves, linked to the plant’s
digital control system, allowed for automatic start-up of the
dedicated pumps with each of the chillers.
Construction occurred during the winter months and UCI
staged the work to allow half of the plant to remain on-line
during the retrofit project.
The university pre-purchased the pumps, two-position con-
trol valves, modulating bypass control valves, and temperature
transmitters. This allowed maximum control over the selection
of the equipment. The university was able to acquire all of the
equipment before releasing a contract for construction. This
eliminated any construction delays due to equipment delivery.
Performance and Energy Savings
The pumps are operating successfully and the measured
power usage is less than or approximately equal to that pre-
dicted with the exception of chiller No. 5. This is likely because
the chilled water pump often is operated at 5,000 gpm
(315 L/s) versus 3,500 gpm (221 L/s).
Before the retrofit work, UCI measured the actual average
power kilowatt draw of each of the existing throttled primary
chilled water pumps. The annual pumping energy cost for the
existing throttled pumps was estimated by multiplying the av-
erage measured electric power by the estimated number of an-
nual operating hours by UCI’s calculated annual average cost
of electricity ($0.06 per kWh based on SCE’s TOU-8 rate sched-
ule). The estimated energy cost for throttled primary chilled
water pump operation was approximately $91,600 per year.
One year after the completion of the project, UCI read the
operating hours and kilowatt-hours from each of the pump’s
VFDs. The average kilowatt draw and the estimated cost of
operation was calculated as approximately $14,600. The esti-
mated first-year savings is approximately $77,000. Table 5 and
Table 6 provide these findings.
Conclusion
This system has provided UCI not only energy savings and
a payback of approximately six years, but also the ability to
better control chiller operation. Now, regardless of the campus
chilled water return temperature and varying header differen-
tial pressures, the central plant operators have the ability to:
•
Keep the chillers fully loaded.
•
Maximize each chiller’s ∆T by setting the minimum evapo-
rator flow rate.
•
Provide the minimum allowable leaving chilled water sup-
ply temperature.
•
Easily operate the chilled water plant without complex
decisions of how many pumps to run.

I
Wire-to-Water Efficiency
Of Pumping Systems
About the Author
By James B. (Burt) Rishel, P.E.
Fellow/Life Member ASHRAE
n the past, wire-to-water efficiency of pumping systems was seldom con-
sidered except for very large pumps where the wire-to-water efficiency
of the pump-motor combination was of significant importance. The ad-
vent of higher electrical costs in conjunction with the emergence of the vari-
able speed drive and digital electronics has made wire-to-water efficiency
invaluable for smaller pumping systems.
Of what value is wire-to-water effi-
ciency? This procedure is the best
method of determining the overall effi-
ciency of a pumping system. It includes
the efficiency of the pump, the pump
motor, that of any variable speed drive
involved, and, on multiple pump systems,
it takes into account the losses in the pip-
ing fittings that surround the pumps. It
enables an engineer or operator to evalu-
ate the total pumping installation. An ex-
tensive review of this efficiency will be
made later.
Basically, wire-to-water efficiency is the
energy that is imparted to the water di-
vided by the energy that came in over the
electrical wires. It is work done divided
by work applied.
The wire-to-water efficiency of a single
pump, constant speed pumping system is:
%

Ep = Pump Efficiency as a fraction
The wire-to-water efficiency of pump-
motor combinations will vary from less
than 20% for small, fractional horsepower,
circulating pumps to more than 85% for
large pumps and motors. Therefore, it is
beneficial to consolidate pumping func-
tions where it is economically feasible.
The wire-to-water efficiency of a mul-
tiple pump, constant speed pumping sys-
tem is:

valve, shut-off valves, and the header
losses that direct the flow into and from
each pump.
The wire-to-water efficiency of a single-
pump, variable-speed pumping system is:

able-speed pumping system with multiple
pumps is:

There may be some question concern-
ing the inclusion of the fitting loss, Hpf ,
in the formula for wire-to-water efficiency
for multiple pumping systems. It must be
included, as it is impossible to program
the pumps on and off on multiple pump-
ing systems without considering the fit-
ting loss, because the flow through each
pump is a fraction of the total system flow.
The loss through the pump fittings af-
fects the overall wire-to-water efficiency.
If pumps are operated above their design
flow, the fitting loss will be much greater
than at design flow. Basically, the loss
through fittings varies generally with the
square of the flow. It is ignored on single-
pump systems, because the flow through
the pump is the same as the total system
flow.
The wire-to-shaft efficiency of the mo-
tor and the variable-speed drive is one of
the most important, and yet, difficult effi-
ciencies to generate. It is not only the
efficiency of the motor multiplied by the
efficiency of the drive. The efficiency of
the motor is dependent upon the load
imposed upon it. The load on a motor at
reduced speed is determined by the sys-
tem head curve or the system head area.
Companies who provide wire-to-water
efficiency calculations understand these
requirements and provide the software for
the calculation of wire-to-water efficiency
once the parameters of the water system
and its pumping equipment have been de-
termined.
Other than the generation of the term,
Ews, the equations for wire-to-water effi-
ciency are fairly straightforward. The cal-
culation of wire-to-water efficiency for a
James B. (Burt) Rishel, P.E., has
formed a consulting company,
Pumping Solutions LLC, Cincinnati.

ASHRAE Journal
prospective system can be difficult if in-
adequate system analysis is used. The
following discussion of a relatively simple
chilled water system will demonstrate this
complexity.
The equation for system head for any
water system is:

it has proved to be a variable. (See the
following explanation.)
System Analysis
For many years, it was assumed that
the system head curve for any installa-
tion had an exponent of two or that the
head varied as the square of the flow.

lations were based upon an exponent of

1.85, and a review of Darcy Wiesbach
Equation friction data indicates an expo-
nent of around 1.90. All of these older
system curves were based upon a uni-
form loading throughout the water sys-
tem. In real life, this is seldom found. A
system head area with the exponent vary-
ing from 0.37 to 3.7 more truly represents
the relationship between the system flow
and system head. So far, this article per-
tains to all water systems. The following
is based upon a loop-type, chilled water
system. This system is described in Fig-
ures 1 through 8.
Assume that the water system is as
shown in Figure 1 for a building with 10
floors of cooling units, each requiring 160
gpm (10 L/s). Also assume that the re-
quired head for each floor cooling unit
coil, control valve, and piping is 25 feet
of head (75 kPa). Further assume that the
friction loss in the building headers is 35
feet (105 kPa). The uniform system head
curve, assuming a coefficient of 1.85, is
25 ft

44
ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
April 2001
ASHRAE Journal
described in Figure 2. This assumes that all 10 floors of cooling
units vary their loads uniformly as shown in Figure 3 at 40%
load on the system.
Now let the load on the system remain at 40% with only the
four bottom floors active as indicated in Figure 4. The overall
friction loss will now be less than that for the uniformly loaded
building.
Next, let the top four floors be fully loaded as shown in Fig-
ure 5. Now the friction loss will be greater than the uniformly
loaded building at 40% load. If this procedure were followed
from one to ten floors, the actual system head area for this
building would be as described in Figure 6. Most buildings do
not have as broad a system area as this.
This is a demonstration of the complexity of system evalua-
tion with a relatively simple building. When a group of build-
ings is evaluated, the calculations become more challenging.
Figure 7 illustrates a small campus of seven buildings divided
into two distribution loops. Assume that the building of Figure
1 does not have an internal chiller plant and is located at two
different points on this installation, namely at Point A and Point
B. The system head area for the building at Point A will be
substantially the same as Figure 6. This will not be the case
when the building is located at Point B.
Assume that the friction loss in the supply and return loops
between the buildings and the central plant is thirty feet at
design load on all of the five buildings. We now have two
conditions to consider for Building B, one with all of the other
four buildings at design load and, secondly, with no load on
these four buildings. The result is Figure 8.
The system head area has broadened appreciably, and
it is obvious that substantial energy will be wasted if
the pump selection and control are not considered carefully.
It may be argued that this is an unusual situation,
but the author has found many similar conditions over the past
30 years.
This type of head variation has been masked in the past by
balancing out any so-called over pressures. The load variation
in the campus-type application will complicate the wire-to-wa-
ter efficiency evaluation. Instead, just the simple building itself
as shown in Figure 1 will be used in the following discussion
of wire-to-water efficiency.
Wire-To-Water Efficiency Analysis
Assume that the above building has its own chilled water
pumps. Three pumps will be used with a 50% capacity each of
800 gpm (50 L/s). The pump head will be the building head of 60
feet (179 kPa) plus the pumping system friction loss, Hpf, of 8
feet (24 kPa) for a total head of 68 feet (203 kPa).
Wire-to-water efficiency calculations will now be run for the
building at system head curve coefficients of 0.37, 0.74, 1.11,
1.48, 1.85, and 2.59, and 3.70 using Equation 4. These exponents
were selected to cover the entire system head area incremen-
tally. This will provide us with the wire-to-water efficiency of
the pumping system with one, two, and three pumps operating
under different load conditions in the building. The actual cal-
culations will not be shown, as there is not room in this short
article for them. Also, the results of the wire-to-water efficiency
evaluation are what should be emphasized here.
The results of the computer runs at the water system expo-
nents listed earlier are summarized in Tables 1 and 2. Table 1
describes the variation in wire-to-water efficiency with changes
in the system head curve exponent while Table 2 indicates the
variation in the number of pumps that should be operated with
changes in the system head curve exponent.
The pumps were selected so that two pumps could operate
this chilled water system at 100% load. Table 2 demonstrates
that although this is possible, it is more efficient to run all three
pumps at loads in excess of from 1,280 to 1,600 gpm (81 to 101
L/s) depending upon the system coefficient.
The Tables 1 and 2 also demonstrate the variation in wire-to-
water efficiency and optimum number of pumps that should be
operated on this particular chilled water system. Figure 9 de-
scribes the variation of the wire-to-water efficiency with the
system head coefficient for one, two, and three pumps operat-
ing. Actually, this is a two dimensional description of three-
dimensional surfaces. Plotting system flow, wire-to-water effi-
ciency, and system head on the x, y, and z axes results in the
three-dimensional surfaces shown in Figure 10.

45
Pumps
How can one possibly develop this technology, practically,
into the actual control of pumps for such a relatively simple
water system, let alone the complexity of a multiple building
system as shown in Figure 7? The advent of digital computer
technology will enable software engineers to generate the equa-
tions for the surfaces shown in Figure 10 and to define the
points of intersection at which pump operation should transi-
tion from one to two to three pumps automatically regardless of
the system head curve coefficient. Practically, plant operators
can program their pumps through the observation of the elec-
trical consumption of the pumping system as loads vary on the
water system. Although no great variations in energy exist for
this model building with three 20 hp (15 kW) pumps, consider
the larger building complexes where as many as six 800 hp (597
kW) pumps are required to distribute many thousands of gal-
lons per minute.
The actual use of wire-to-water efficiency in the operation of
water systems has proved to be of great value for operating
engineers. By using Equation 5, the operating wire-to-water
efficiency of a water system can be displayed on the operator’s
monitor where it can be checked to ensure that the correct
number of pumps is in operation. Any of the techniques of

information storage, retrieval, and manipulation can be used to
ensure that the pumping system is operating at peak, overall
efficiency. Past performance comparisons can be made to deter-
mine the need for pump service and inspection. A particular
installation where this technology is used is the central chilled
water plant at Harvard University.
The transition points in multiple pump systems are very im-
portant in pumping a water system efficiently, and they can be
checked easily to ensure that the calculated program is adequate
for the actual system. This eliminates operation at low and high
flows in the individual pumps. This, in turn, reduces noise and
unnecessary wear in the pumps themselves. The pumps are
operated as closely as possible to their best efficiency curve.
Variable speed pumping systems using this technology are
known to operate for many years without any structural dam-
age to the pumps themselves.
This demonstrates that wire-to-water efficiency technology
is useful through digital computer programming for even rela-
tively small systems. As more companies become proficient in
this software, it will become standard for any water system with
multiple loads and total pump horsepower in excess 50 hp (37
kW). The previously mentioned variation in wire-to-water effi-
ciency for individual pump-motor combinations from less than

January 2001
ASHRAE Journal
S193
ASHRAE Journal
Quality
I
Achieving Quality
Using TQM and ISO
About the Author
By Gordon V.R. Holness
Fellow ASHRAE
Gordon V.R. Holness is chairman
and CEO of Albert Kahn Associates.
He is a member of ASHRAE’s Publish-
ing Council.
n a increasingly competitive marketplace, providing a quality product,
delivered on time and within budget, is seen as a prerequisite for success-
ful business practice. Consistent delivery of quality only can be achieved
by using repeatable and sustainable practices. Performance at this level can
help each of us address our ultimate business goal of being our clients first
choice in what is truly becoming a globally competitive marketplace.
In the design profession, the smaller,
proprietor-led companies typically
achieve quality through dedicated per-
sonal attention and service, undertaking
each element of design. However, as com-
panies grow larger in size and diversity
of practice, and handle larger projects,
this personal attention and service be-
comes harder to achieve on a consistent
and repeatable basis throughout the com-
pany. A large industrial or health care
project may involve anywhere from 50 to
150 team members expending 10,000 to
50,000 labor-hours over a 6 to 18-month
period, requiring a more structured sys-
tem, with individual commitment within a
team approach.
This article reviews the evolution of
Total Quality Management philosophies
and their progression into structured
quality operating systems such as the
international ISO standards and their po-
tential impact on ASHRAE members. It
addresses the feasibility of such struc-
tured programs for professional engineer-
ing services and in the manufacturing of
equipment.
Total Quality Management
Total Quality Management (TQM) pro-
grams have existed for many years in a
variety of forms. Many are the offspring
of Dr. William Edwards Demings
doctrinations to the Japanese in the
1950s and 60s. Subsequent quality gu-
rus, such as Crosby, Juran, Hayden and
others, have trumpeted the message that
quality is the key to corporate success.
Deming1,2 established 14 points for man-
agement (Table 1). Crosby3,4 suggested
14 steps to quality improvement (Table
2). During this period, the definition of
quality has evolved, e.g., 1970s conform
to product requirements; 1980s con-
form to customers expectations; and
1990s exceed customers expectations.
TQM is a philosophy and a set of guid-
ing principles that represent the founda-
tion of a continuously improving
organization, leading to the elimination
of waste. By design, TQM is the applica-
tion of quantitative methods and human
resources to improve 1) the material and
services provided by an organization, 2)
all the processes within an organization
and 3) the degree to which the needs of
the client are met, now and in the future.
TQM integrates fundamental manage-
ment techniques, existing improvement
efforts, and technical tools under a disci-
plined approach focused on continuous
improvement. It is also the implementa-
tion of the guiding principles of Kaizen
Teian Zguiding improvements through
employee suggestions.
The approach transfers the ownership
of work to those who execute the work.
This may sound simplistic, but in larger
organizations it is often complex. Lead-
ers must create an environment of own-
ership and employee empowerment so
that each person wants to be responsible
for his or her own performance. This is
well addressed in the book, Zapp  The
Lightning of Empowerment,5 on how to
improve quality, productivity and em-
ployee satisfaction.
People feel powerless when they expe-
rience no control over their work. Lead-
ers can coach the development of
individuals capabilities and competence.
Coaches can help people see beyond
where they are now and to what they can
become. Being empowered and respon-
sible for our performance, as well as our
team performance, often is contrary to our
traditional roles in a hierarchical organi-
zation and requires a change in mind set.
It inverts organizational pyramids to be-
come employee-driven and employee-
empowered. This is explained well in The
Flight of the Buffalo: Soaring to Excel-
lence, Learning to Let Employees Lead6
and in Teaching the Elephant to Dance.7
ISO: The Next Step
Recently, various quality certification
programs have been promoted. The In-
ternational Organization for Standardiza-
tion (ISO) in Geneva, Switzerland has
ASHRAE Journal
S194
www.pub-serv.com/rs/ashrae
J a n u a r y 2 0 0 1
developed a series of standards for in-
dustry. Emanating out of Europe, ISO has
60 member countries. It is rapidly becom-
ing the predominant standard for the au-
tomotive and aircraft industries. ISO
standards such as ISO.9000 (1987) cover
quality management and quality assur-
ance guidelines for manufacturing com-
panies, while ISO.9001 (1994) provides
similar guidelines related to service indus-
tries and provides a model for quality as-
surance in design, development,
production, installation and servicing.
Companion standards include ISO 9002
(1994), which addresses production, in-
stallation and servicing; ISO 9003 (1994)
addresses final inspection and testing;
ISO 14001 addresses environmental man-
agement. Although ISO has gained wide-
spread acceptance in Europe and Asia, it
is only now beginning to make signifi-
cant inroads in the United States.
ISO 9001 offers a documentation struc-
ture supporting a set of policies and pro-
cedures guiding the design, development,
production, and delivery of manufactured
product, as well as delivery of services
(design, educational, research and oth-
ers). As such, it offers guidelines for most
segments of ASHRAEs membership. ISO,
itself, does not ensure quality, but it does
establish a structure for consistent deliv-
ery of product and services. Quality Op-
erating Systems (QOS) can serve as the
basis for continuous improvement and,
when coupled with TQM programs, can
significantly improve not only quality but
also productivity. Taken together, ISO/
TQM can move corporations forward in
a complementary and dramatic way to
improve quality.
The questions for manufacturers, ser-
vice industries, educators and engineers
today are:

What are the benefits of these
programs?

Do they really improve quality and
efficiency?

Is the effort to implement the
programs worthwhile?

Can the investment and mainte-
nance costs be justified?

Is this just the latest management
fad, philosophy and marketing
gimmick?
Even if the answers to these questions
are affirmative, do potential downsides
to these efforts exist? Certainly! When
owners insist on manufacturers and de-
signers meeting a certification standard,
the first question to ask is has the client
embraced and implemented the same stan-
dards in their own operations? If not,
then they may have unrealistic and
unachievable expectations. It is important
to select your clients and understand their
Table 1: Deming’s 14 points for management.
characteristics and needs. It is also im-
portant to advise clients of the qualifica-
tions and limitations in what you will do.
Is there increased liability exposure with
or without a TQM/ISO-based quality op-
erating system and quality assurance pro-
gram? The answer could be yes either
way. Without such programs, you could
be subject to damaging testimony as to
Table 2: Crosby’s 14 steps to quality improvement.
e
s
o
p
r
u

Quality
January 2001
ASHRAE Journal’s Official Product & Show Guide
S195
the absence of quality assurance contributing towards your
assumed negligence and performance below the standard of
care of the industry. On the other hand, with ISO/TQM you
must be certain that you do what you say. Absence of docu-
mentary evidence of compliance with your own quality plan
will leave you even more vulnerable, regardless of relativity to
the issue. Above all, be realistic in what you promise and take
care of what you say in promotional materials. Attorneys tell me
that their favorite sources of damaging information are over-
blown promises in marketing materials and on home web page
sites. While we would never accept a contract requiring the
highest standard of professional practice (unattainable), we
often make that promise to our clients in our marketing.
Our Experience
First, recognize that with any of these programs fundamental
changes in philosophy, management style and organizational
culture must occur. As a large to mid-sized (400 person) archi-
tectural and engineering organization, with a legacy going back
more than 100 years, our company well knows the difficulty in
changing corporate culture. The tangible elements are relatively
easy to address, it is the intangibles that are hard to recognize
and even harder to change. It starts by recognizing people,
their changing cultures and needs, and by understanding who
they are. From the Great Depression-sensitive, workaholic, World
War II leaders of today, to the fast-rising Baby Boomers now
taking over, to the free-wheeling Generation Xers entering the
workforce, a real diversity exists and along with that a chal-
lenge in communication, priorities and direction.
Our company started our Total Quality Service (TQS) Pro-
gram eight years ago at a time that we were successful and
reasonably content with the quality and content of our prod-
uct. With the help of a consultant, we spent considerable effort
in internal training and development of our staff and under-
standing of TQS philosophies and culture. Probable cost was
in excess of $1.75 million over a period of three years. Along the
way, we became far more customer-focused, developed better
client relations and services, established a wide range of bench-
marking tools, including issuing client surveys on every project
and conducting internal client surveys with our staff. We were
the first AE worldwide to achieve Ford Motor Companys Q1
Quality Certification in 1995, but not before our first audit showed
that we had no idea of the realities of establishing a formal,
documented quality system.
As a result of the TQS program, we changed our entire orga-
nizational structure, moved away from a technical department-
driven, internally focused operation, to an externally driven
market focus group operation with studio teams and team cen-
ters. We revised our operations by eliminating top-down-driven
corporate board committees and moving to a company cross-
sectional TQS steering committee with process improvement
teams to drive change. We moved on towards QSA-S/QOS as a
common standard for automotive OEMs. We have completed
the final lap and were successful in obtaining ISO 9001 Certifi-
cation in November 1998 by learning from each step in the
process as we progressed through our gap analysis, pre-as-
sessment audit, preliminary audit and initial audit. Is this the
end? No, its just the beginning and we have a long way to go
with our continuous improvement program.
Current expenditure between TQS and ISO is probably about
$2.25 million in personnel time and training. This is a huge in-
vestment for a company of our size. Has this expenditure been
worthwhile? I believe so. Our corporate profitability has steadily
and significantly improved (by more than 20%) over the past
five years. We are in the final phase of an extensive and expen-
sive program to redo our facilities (at a cost of $4.5 million) to
accommodate and respond to these changes. This included
modular and flexible workstations and the establishment of stu-
dio teams and project team centers with all technical disciplines
located together.
As indicated previously, ISO, itself, does not ensure quality.
It can be a valuable tool and when combined with TQS to de-
velop Quality Operating Systems (QOS) can be quite effective.
While TQS develops the philosophy and culture of an organi-
zation, ISO provides the structure to build upon. The ISO 9001
model for quality assurance is driven by 20 clearly defined re-
quirements to establish a QOS (Table 3). It provides focus,
discipline and defined responsibilities. It can provide a struc-
tured basis to develop systematic programs for improvement.
More than anything else, it forces you to document your poli-
cies and procedures for operations. Computer technology has
helped enormously, allowing organized filing and retrieval of all
documented data. The electronic file becomes the controlled
document, providing the latest approved record of current poli-
cies and procedures. All paper documents are uncontrolled
data since it is impossible to determine if they are the latest
official issue. How often do you dig outdated documents from
your file drawer for reference or use on a project?
With ISO there are no halfway measures, its all or nothing.
However, a lot of irrationalities exist in ISO for service compa-
nies that need to be worked out with your auditor. Beaumont8, 9
provides a good insight into ISO requirements and interpreta-
tion. After you get through policies and procedures, large philo-
sophical issues need to be worked out within your organization
as to how far to go in developing policies, procedures and work
instructions. There are the engineers who want to document
and record everything, and then there are the creative archi-
tects who want to do things by instinct. Finding the right bal-
ance is essential, but the system does provide for substantial
flexibility in its application.
The key for success is to fully integrate TQM into daily func-
tions, establishing measurable performance goals, appointing
champions for implementation and monitoring, education and
training as a critical component and establishing benchmarks
for improvement. This involves full management commitment.
It cannot be a flavor of the month. It must create lasting solu-
tions to age-old problems. Benchmarks can range from tradi-
tional business measurables of workload and profitability, to
items like errors and omissions. These are tracked not only to
follow the trends, but also to compare our performance against
industry leaders. Vital benchmarks relate to internal client sur-
veys that address communication and recognition issues, and
external client surveys that address performance expectations
and results. A good presentation on TQM is given by Stephen
ASHRAE Journal
S196
www.pub-serv.com/rs/ashrae
J a n u a r y 2 0 0 1
Covey in Implementing TQM  Competing in the Nineties
through Total Quality Management.10
Key Issues
One of the key issues in a QOS is that of checking the draw-
ings for errors and omissions. The old policy of third-party
checking gave way to the philosophy of do it right the first
time and self-directed checking, that recognized that up-
stream prevention costs less than downstream correction or
repair. In this regard, it is important to understand that 20% to
40% of your employees time is spent redoing a task. There
needs to be two layers of checking/review. The first relates to
quality assurance and here, clearly, the project designer and
design team are in the best position to ensure compliance with
criteria and coordination of technical disciplines. The second
relates to risk management review and should be conducted by
a senior manager looking at broader, fundamental application
and design issues from a risk or liability standpointwill the
system selected or configured meet the requirements and per-
form?
Are we there yet? Not by half! We are just beginning to see
the potential. It has been a huge effort with dedicated key re-
sources to make it happen. But we can answer some of the
questions already.
What are the benefits? Clearly ISO can improve the opera-
tions of service companies by:

Development of an integrated and structured operating
system.

Quality
January 2001
ASHRAE Journal’s Official Product & Show Guide
S197

Disciplined problem solving.

Measured performance, analysis and corrective action.

Elimination of loose practices.

Eradication of wasted work.

Improved documentation of policies and procedures.

Establishment of empowered cross-functional teams.

Defined roles for each team member.

Dynamic and systematic approach that is logical and re-
sults orientated.
Lessons Learned
Insist on providing construction follow-up services. They
are essential to control the services needed to ensure the suc-
cessful completion of a contract.

Look at the role of the project manager in the quality con-
trol program. The project manager is really the gatekeeper.

Hire dedicated and qualified staff to spearhead QA and
T&PD Programs. Do not try to give this to someone as a
secondary role.

Involve the project manager and the team in the proposal
phase of a project so that they really understand the scope
of work.

Allocate resources, set priorities, empower the team, stay
informed but get out of the way.

Hire good people and expect nothing but the best for
them.

Empower the teamthe leadership role is to set direction,
not to make all the decisions.

Get feedback from your employees along the way to make
sure they understand the goals and you understand their
concerns.

Measure and broadcast your successes.

Choose your TQM consultant and your ISO auditor from
companies familiar with service providers, not just manu-
facturing companies.

Be patient, things will get worse before they get better.

Be consistent, persistent, people will look for reasons not
to follow the programs if they see you wavering.
Conclusions
So, back to the original question: Should ASHRAE members
move from an embrace of TQM programs to the next level of
incorporating ISO standards? Grammatically one should not
answer a question with a question, but here goes:

Can these systems really improve quality and efficiency
on a consistent basis? Unequivocally Yes! Our external
client surveys show steady and positive improvements in
all areas. Our internal benchmarking also shows steady
improvements in production efficiency.

Are these programs only applicable to large companies or
should smaller firms also participate? I believe they are
applicable regardless of company size. Establishing cor-
porate design standards and standard details, for example,
avoids repeating the same mistake over again and makes
it easier to bring new staff up to speed with company
practices.

Is it a worthwhile investment to make? Only if you
are totally committed and do it properly. I believe that a
combined TQS/QOS approach is essential. Whether you
go all the way to ISO depends on your client base.
But, it does have the untapped potential to eliminate waste,
provides a foundation for continuous improvement
and provides the measurables to track your progress.

Do these ISO certification programs lead to new clients
or markets? Maybe! But it depends on who they are.
Industrialpossibly, particularly for European companies,
but other marketsnot really. Only a limited demand
exists for this type of qualification. However, we do
know that our TQS/QI efforts have gotten us two or more
new clients.

Would we do it again? Absolutely, but I hope that
we would learn from our mistakes because we have
made many. We understand the process better and
would approach the process in a more deliberate and
aggressive way than we did. This assumes, however, that
changes to our culture could keep pace. (By the way,
the limitation is in the leadership ranks. Employees gener-
ally are quite flexible if a clear and understandable direc-
tion is set.)

The future of certification programs? ISO is not a
panacea for quality improvement. Indeed, it can be a
real challenge to implement. However, when effectively
used in conjunction with a meaningful quality assurance
program, they can have a significant positive impact
on your company. As such, I think the market will con-
tinue to support them, and our clients will demand them.
Final note: the International Standards Organization, like most
professional societies, is constantly striving to update its stan-
dards and terms of reference. ISO is no different, typically oper-
ating on a five-year life cycle, and a revised series of 9000
standards are to be issued shortly. Standard 9001 is being
streamlined and tailored more specifically to the service indus-
tries and users will find it easier to follow and more effective to
implement.
References
Walton, M. and W. Edwards Deming. 1988. The Deming Man-
agement Method.
Aquayo, R. and W. Edwards Deming. 1991. Dr. Deming: The
Man Who Taught the Japanese about Quality.
Crosby, P.B. 1992. Quality is Free: The Art of Making Qual-
ity Certain.
Crosby, P.B. 1989. Quality without Tears: The Art of Quality
for Management.
Byham, J.C. and Jeff Cox. 1992. Zapp The Lightning of Em-
powerment.
Belasco, J.A. and R.C. Stayer. 1993. Flight of the Buffalo:
Soaring to Excellence, Learning to Let Employees Lead.
Belasco, J.A. 1991. Teaching the Elephant to Dance.
Beaumont, L.R. 1993. ISO 9001 The Standard Companion.
Beaumont, L.R. 1995. ISO 9001 The Standard Interpretation.
Covey, S.R. and J.R. Jablonski. 1994. Implementing TQM:
Competing in the Nineties through Total Quality
Management.
ASHRAE Journal
Management
38
ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
J a n u a r y 2 0 0 1
R
Leading Engineers,
Not Just Managing Them
About the Author
By Robert D. Lehman III, P.E.
Member ASHRAE
Robert D. Lehman III, P.E., is an en-
gineering manager with Hoshizaki,
America, Peachtree City, Ga.
etaining good engineers is critical to the success of you as a man-
ager and to your company. Now is a great time for an employed
engineer to look for a new job. Technology is rapidly evolving,
which is resulting in many exciting opportunities. International economies
appear to be on the rebound from their difficulties. Demand for engineers is
high and supply is low, which leads to strong financial incentives to change
jobs. Recruiters are in a feeding frenzy, trying to steal the best engineers and
place them in another firm for a big payday. In todays environment, an
engineer does not need to actively look for a new job. You could lose an
essential engineer who simply listens to an idea proposed by a recruiter.
Several factors contribute to an
engineers job satisfaction. One often-
overlooked factor is the effectiveness of
the leadership above the engineer. Com-
panies with good engineering leadership
retain more engineers than companies
without. Although this seems obvious,
many engineering managers do not spend
enough time thinking about effective lead-
ership (as opposed to management) as a
retention tool. This article discusses five
ways for engineering managers, supervi-
sors, directors, and vice presidents to
transition from managing to leading,
thereby increasing their ability to retain
essential engineers.
Earning the Right to Lead
Managers need certain prerequisites to
effectively lead. These include a techni-
cal education and several years of pro-
gressive engineering experience, and per-
haps an advanced degree or MBA. These
accomplishments might help you earn the
opportunity to manage, but not the right
to lead.
To earn the right to lead, managers first
must be worthy of respect. Good leaders
are good citizens of the company and set
good examples. Leaders have personal
and professional traits that engineers can
admire and model, including time manage-
ment, communication, patience, goal set-
ting, and the ability to work well with oth-
ers. Good leaders also have demonstrated
their ability to lead smaller projects, bring
together different people from different
departments, and complete projects on
time and on budget. They have devel-
oped a history of success, and have had
experiences that helped to develop and
improve their project management skills.
Effective leaders recognize that their
engineers may know more about certain
subjects. This is especially true when en-
gineers have a longer tenure with the com-
pany than the manager. Therefore, leaders
spend time expanding their knowledge. The
leader of an engineering team does not
need to be the smartest engineer, or know
the most. The leaders role is to recognize
individual strengths, combine engineers
into a good team, and lead them so the
team is greater than the sum of its parts.
Finally, to earn the right to lead, the
leader must be involved with the personal
and professional successes of the engi-
neers. The effective leader must truly care
about the engineers. This cannot be
faked, and any attempt to do so will
quickly harm the managers credibility.
Leaders help people grow and achieve
their goals. Effective leaders are mentors
to their engineers.
Always Lead by Example
Everything the engineering manager
does is leading by example. Leaders ac-
tively set good, positive, consistent ex-
amples for engineers to model. Engineers
watch every move of their superiors, and
use these observations to define what is
acceptable and what is not. This includes
dealing well with people, being punctual
to meetings, respecting the time and opin-
ions of others, achieving deadlines, writ-
ing reports, talking on the phone, spend-
ing time off-task, and everything else that
the boss does (or does not do).
If you call your relatives and talk for 30
minutes, this is leading by example. If you
put your feet on your desk and read a
report, this is leading by example. If you
act politely to someone you disagree
with, this is leading by example. If you
surf the net looking for a new home com-
puter or car, this is leading by example.
You are constantly leading by example.
If you are inconsistent, your engineers
will notice and copy this inconsistency.
Effective leaders set good, consistent
examples all the time.
It is important to remember that if you
want your engineers to do something,
you must also do it yourself. This in-
cludes providing regular status reports,
meeting with people, showing patience,
meeting your own deadlines, and ac-
counting for your time. If you think a task
is so menial or trivial that you do not have
January 2001
ASHRAE Journal
39
Management
to do it, do not assign the same task to
your engineer. The engineer will recog-
nize a do what I say, not what I do atti-
tude and you will lose your credibility and
status as a leader. If a task is important
enough for an engineer to do, it is impor-
tant enough for the leader, too.
Listen, Listen, Listen
Listening is the most important aspect
of communication, yet many managers are
so interested in giving direction and
speaking that they overlook the need to
gather and interpret data. To be a great
leader, gather as much information as
possible, from as many different perspec-
tives as possible, and then mold them
together to develop clear, effective direc-
tion. It is far more important for a leader
to listen than for a leader to speak.
A truly great leader, when thrust into a
new situation, asks probing questions
that get to the heart of the issues, and
listens carefully to the answers. Some-
times it is necessary only to listen for sev-
eral days or weeks before an appropriate
response can be given. If a leader does
not listen or does not ask questions to
get the right data, the leader will be prone
to making inappropriate decisions and
statements, which will quickly return the
leader to manager status.
When listening, a leader must hear what
is trying to be said, as opposed to inter-
preting the words literally. Many engi-
neers struggle with clearly articulating an
issue, so the leader must listen to the in-
tent and carefully watch the non-verbal
communication. Leaders hear what the
communicator means, not what the com-
municator says. Leaders also overlook
individual idiosyncrasies, and accept in-
formation without bias.
Say “Yes” Often
Most engineers have many great ideas
and are enthusiastic, and want to improve
their product and company. The engineer-
ing leaders role is to find ways to allow
the engineers to make improvements, and
remove obstacles in their paths. One great
way to accomplish this is to find a way to
always say yes. Even if the manager does
not agree completely, or can think of some
problems with an idea, the leader still al-
lows the engineer to outline the project or
begin working on a project that has been
effectively outlined. This way the engineer
is working on the problem, discovering the
hurdles, and developing potential coun-
termeasures. If the manager says no, he
has squashed the engineers initiative and
decreased the likelihood of receiving more
ideas. If an engineer has given up making
proposals for fear of constant rejection,
that engineer will be more apt to listen to
those enticing offers from the friendly re-
cruiter who calls every day with an op-
portunity that would be great for some-
one just like you.
If an engineer is too busy to work on
an idea, approve it anyway. Be clear that
Advertisement for the print edition formerly in this space.
40
ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
J a n u a r y 2 0 0 1
ASHRAE Journal
existing deadlines and goals for higher
priority projects still must be met. Do not
focus on the time burden of the extra
project. Rather, focus on meeting the ex-
isting, clearly identified and agreed-upon
goals and deadlines. The engineer might
find a few minutes to work on the addi-
tional project, becoming more efficient
and productive in the process (and prob-
ably happier, too).
By saying yes to an idea or a pro-
posal, you are building up the engineers
confidence. If the project turns out to be
a good one, you likely will have saved
the company money and improved your
product or process. You will also have a
better, more aggressive engineer who is
developing a record of successes. If you
approve the project and the engineer dis-
covers too many problems to succeed,
you have developed your engineers
problem-solving skills and confidence. At
the same time, you have improved the
likelihood of receiving more ideas from
that engineer.
In the rare event that you must reject
an idea and the engineer disagrees, try to
find small ways to work toward solving
or understanding the problem. Perhaps
you need to suggest a different path. Or,
suggest a fact-finding mission to help the
engineer discover why you rejected the
idea. If you do this, you have effectively
converted a rejection into a positive ac-
tion, or found a way to say yes.
Accept Different Styles
As an engineer advances up the cor-
porate ladder, it is difficult to recognize
that not everyone works the same way.
This is difficult because your style works
well; otherwise, you would not be advanc-
ing. As an engineer becomes a manager, it
is an easy temptation to insist that every-
one work your way. This includes styles
of writing memos, handling projects, com-
municating, giving presentations, etc. Ef-
fective leaders understand and recognize
the difference between style and philoso-
phy. Leaders recognize that more than one
right way exists, and that personal style
differences are fine so long as the leaders
philosophy is followed. It is the content,
tone, and clarity of communication that
really matters, not the style.
Avoid rewriting engineers memos and
letters, or redesigning presentations
based on style preference instead of con-
tent. You might point out areas that are
unclear or ambiguous or send the wrong
message, but do not do a complete re-
write. This would send a message of lack
of trust, lack of faith, and micro-manage-
ment. If an engineers style is not working
because of a major inconsistency with the
company, it is your job as a leader to work
with the engineer on this inconsistency.
This takes a lot of time and effort.
Recognizing different individual styles
and embracing the differences helps to
be viewed as a leader. Accepting other
styles will also increase engineers desire
to ask for your input into their work.
Following these five steps will help any
manager evolve into an effective leader.
Advertisement for the print edition formerly in this space.

ASHRAE Journal
2 5
Darrell Craig Mays is a control sys-
tems engineer at Honeywell, Torrance,
Calif.
Anthony B. Campbell, Ph.D., is a
control systems engineer at
Honeywell, Torrance, Calif.
Satish S. Nair, Ph.D., P.E., is a pro-
fessor, Department of Mechanical and
Aerospace Engineering University of
Missouri-Columbia.
John B. Miles, Ph.D., P.E., is an
emeritus professor, University of Mis-
souri-Columbia.
Gretchen A. Thomas is an employee
of NASA Johnson Space Center,
Houston.
S
About the Authors
By Darrell Craig Mays, Anthony B. Campbell, Ph.D.,
Satish S. Nair, Ph.D., P.E., John B. Miles, Ph.D., P.E.,
Member ASHRAE Associate Member ASHRAE
and Gretchen A. Thomas
ustaining life in the harsh vacuum of space and in the low-pressure
carbon dioxide Martian atmosphere is challenging and complex.
Additionally, there exists a large range of effective temperatures,
and the ever-present danger of micrometeorite and space debris impact.
On Mars, as on Earth, wide climate differences exist at different points on
the planet and temperatures vary by several degrees even from the astronaut’s
feet to head levels. Astronauts have reported cold discomfort during Extra-
vehicular Activities (EVAs), which distracted them from their work.1 The
Martian atmosphere is made up of mostly carbon dioxide at a pressure of
0.123 psia (848.1 Pa), far less than the Earth’s atmospheric pressure of
14.7 psia (101.4 kPa). These conditions must be quantified so engineers
can develop and optimize a space suit system for a given task. The primary
factors to be considered are:2
1. The human body produces anywhere
from 400 Btu/h (117.3 W) of heat during
light activity to 2,000 Btu/h (586.5 W)3
under strenuous conditions. Anywhere
from 60% to 80% of the total heat gener-
ated is sensible heat with the remaining
20% to 40% being latent heat. This heat
and moisture must be removed to main-
tain an astronaut’s thermal comfort and
thermal equilibrium.
2. Based on the assumption that space
is a true radiative heat sink, the tempera-
ture of objects in space can range from
near absolute zero in shaded conditions
to 220°F (104.4°C) in the direct line of sight
of the sun.3 While most EVAs take place
within the 0 to 70°F (–17.78 to 21.1°C) ef-
fective temperature range, the Extrave-
hicular Mobile Unit (EMU), or space suit,
must be robust enough to account for
the largest variations for brief periods of
time. The effective temperature is the av-
0.009 psia (4.8 mmHg). A much lower suit
pressure than the Earth’s atmospheric
pressure is used to allow adequate mo-
bility in the suit (3.7 psia [25.5 kPa] to 8.3
psia [64.1 kPa]). At these lower pressures,
pure oxygen must be used to compen-
sate for the astronaut’s ability to absorb
the oxygen in the lungs. A pre-breathe
time is necessary to denitrogenate the
blood to prevent the bends. 3
5. The suit must have adequate mobil-
ity to ensure the completion of tasks with
minimal exertion by the astronaut.
6. All systems must be compatible with
the host system, whether it is the shuttle,
a space station, or a planetary habitat.
7. Crew safety is a paramount issue.
Redundant systems or high reliability
components must be implemented and
emergency procedures must be gener-
ated to cover a wide variety of problems
(e.g., decompression, loss of power).
This article provides a fundamental
technical review of the thermal technolo-
gies associated with former, current, and
planned future space suits. It discusses
erage temperature seen by the suit due to
radiation from bodies in space and radia-
tion to deep space. The Martian atmo-
sphere is mostly CO2 at a pressure of
0.123 psia (848.1 Pa) and temperatures can
be anywhere from –189°F (–123.2°C) to
67.7°F (19.9°C). High winds around 45
mph (76 km/h) that cause large convec-
tive heat transfer rates and a large solar
flux that causes high radiation loads must
also be considered in any design.4
3. The suit outer garment must be strong
enough to withstand micrometeorite im-
pacts at velocities of 25,000 mph (40 233
km/h), and it also must be able to with-
stand large dust storms.
4. Oxygen must be supplied and car-
bon dioxide must be removed from the
suit interior. At a metabolic rate of 1,000
Btu/h, the typical oxygen consumption
rate is 0.1626 lbm/hr (0.0738 kg/h), which
increases the partial pressure of CO2 by
Thermal Technologies
For Space Suits
2 6
ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
J a n u a r y 2 0 0 1
ASHRAE Journal
the predecessor of the shuttle EMU
(Figure 1), the Apollo suit, and explores
its contemporary, the Russian Orlan
DMA.
The article then compares them to the
two new suit designs, the Minimum
Consumables Portable Life Support Sys-
tem (MPLSS) (Figure 2), and the Cryo-
genic oxygen Portable Life Support Sys-
tem (CPLSS) (Figure 3). The article also
highlights the advantages of the new
designs in terms of performance and
consumable usage.
Heat Rejection and Addition
Due to the large range of effective
temperatures in space and the wide
range of atmospheric temperatures seen
on Mars, heating and cooling are re-
quired to maintain astronaut thermal
comfort. However, due to the low overall thermal conductivity
of the SSA (0.11 Btu/ft2·F, 0.0697 kJ/m2·K) and the environmen-
tal limitations imposed on most missions, cooling the astronaut
is the primary goal of the PLSS designs. In fact, the Apollo and
DMA designs have no heating elements at all and the Shuttle
EMU has heating only in the gloves because insulation must
be minimized to provide maximum hand mobility and dexterity.
The MPLSS and the CPLSS will have active heating systems
but they are expected to be used sparingly. Table 1 shows the
maximum and minimum heat leaks for the shuttle suit in an Earth
orbit and for the two new designs in the Martian environment
given later.
For the shuttle suit, because no active heating elements ex-
ist, thermal comfort cannot be maintained in a cold environment
with a low metabolic rate for extended periods of time. Typi-
cally, mission planning has sought to avoid this situation, al-
though cold discomfort has been reported. The two new de-
signs require active heating for the most extreme cold condi-
tions and low metabolic rate. However, with an average meta-
bolic rate of 940 Btu/h (275 W) in moderate activity only cool-
ing will be required even in cold conditions.
Typical Martian Environmental Conditions4
Atmospheric Temperature = –72.4°F (–58°C)
Solar Flux = 150 Btu/hr·ft2 (473.2 W/m2)
Atmospheric Pressure = 0.123 psia (848.1 Pa)
Wind Speed = 33 ft/s (10.1 m/s)
Metabolic Rate = 938.3 Btu/h (275 W)
Primary Heat Rejection. To remove the heat from the astro-
naut, each of the SSA designs have a Ventilation Loop (VL) and
a Water Cooling Loop (WCL). The VL is the space between the
outer suit garment and the astronaut’s body where the oxygen
flows over the astronaut’s head and is circulated back to the
PLSS through ducts at the wrists and ankles.
While the VL provides the breathing gas and maintains suit
pressure, it also is responsible for removing the latent heat from
the astronaut and a small portion of the sensible heat produced.
Both combine to account for roughly 20% of the total heat
production of the astronaut with a constant entering dew point
temperature of 40°F (4.4°C). Because the CPLSS design has a
variable entering dew point temperature (20°F to 35°F or –6.67°C
to 16.67°C), the latent load can account for up to 50% of the
astronaut’s metabolic rate.
The predecessor of the Apollo, the Gemini suit, attempted to
use only the VL to remove all of the heat from the astronaut. This
proved ineffective, which led to the development of the WCL.
The WCL is responsible for removing the remaining heat
from the astronaut. In each of the designs, a spandex garment,
called the Liquid Cooling Garment (LCG) (Figure 4), has small
tubes woven into the fabric that circulate the cooling water
over the astronaut’s torso, arms, and legs. These tubes essen-
tially act as a heat exchanger between the astronaut’s skin and
the PLSS by removing or adding heat from the astronaut via
convection and conduction. Then, the water is circulated back
to the PLSS for rejection to space.
Removing the heat from the astronaut is only half of the

January 2001
ASHRAE Journal
2 7
Thermal Technology
problem; the heat must also be rejected to space or to a plan-
etary environment. The Apollo suit, the shuttle PLSS, and the
DMA, all use a variation of what NASA calls a Sublimator2,3 to
reject the heat. This device (Figure 5) uses a feedwater tank
with expendable water that flows in a separate water circuit
than the WCL and is exposed to space through a porous plate.
When the feedwater comes into contact with the vacuum of
space, it begins to vaporize and this heat transfer cools the LCG
water and the VL air. It is called a sublimator instead of an
evaporator because in a cold vacuum environment, the water
first freezes and then sublimates to space. In case too much
cooling is being provided, a three-way manually operated Tem-
perature Control Valve (TCV) is used to bypass the cooling
water around the sublimator.
Because this device operates based on the sublimation of
water, the pure vacuum of space and a cold effective tempera-
ture of the sublimator plate are required. At full flow, the shuttle
sublimator is capable of rejecting up to 2,800 Btu/h (821.1 W) of
total heat from the water and vent loops. The sublimator also is
used to condense the water out of the vent stream. This water
collects into a slurper and is added to the feedwater or rejected
to space.
One of the main problems with the sublimator is that the
feedwater used to drive the system is a major expendable. The
tanks contain 9.87 lbs (4.48 kg) of water. Another problem is
that without ultra-clean feedwater, the porous plate can be-
come fouled with contaminants and its thermal rejection char-
acteristics will deteriorate. Contaminated plates must be recon-
ditioned or replaced with new sublimator plates, which NASA
wants to avoid for long-term missions.
Figure 6 shows the shuttle PLSS sublimator heat rejection
capabilities versus the TCV position used. As the flow through
the sublimator is increased, its overall UA value is increased
and its heat rejection capability goes up quadratically. The heat
transfer between the sublimator and space can be described by
Figure 4: Liquid
cooling
gar-
ment (LCG) with
shuttle suit.
Figure 5: Shuttle sublimator schematic.
Figure 1: Shuttle suit schematic.
Figure 2: MPLSS schematic.
Figure 3: CPLSS schematic.
2 8
ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
J a n u a r y 2 0 0 1
ASHRAE Journal
ponents and to develop automatic
control strategies to regulate ther-
mal comfort. The equations used
to describe the heat transfer in the
radiator are:
rad,plate = rad,env + solar
pipe = rad,plate + cond,gas
H2O = mH2OCP,H2O(Tout,H2O – Tin,H2O)
+ UA (Tpipe – Tout,H2O)
where,
rad,env
= Rate of heat transfer to
the radiator plate from
the environment.
rad,plate = Rate of radiation from
the environment.
solar
= Rate of solar absorp-
tion.
pipe
= Rate of heat transfer from the
plate to the water pipe.
cond,gas = Rate of heat transfer via con-
duction through the gas gap.
H2O
= Rate of heat transfer from the
water pipe to the water.
UA
= Overall heat transfer coeffi-
cient between the pipe and H2O.
Secondary Heat Rejection. The second-
ary heat rejection component for the
MPLSS is the Water Boiler (WB).4 The WB
is similar to the sublimator in that it relies
on the vaporization of water to remove heat
from the LCG water. The idea is to use it
only when the environment is too warm or
the metabolic rate too high for the radia-
tor. A significant part of the design is that

(1)
where,
space
= the heat rejected to space
by sublimation.
mfw
= the mass flow rate of the
feedwater.

= the change in enthalpy of

For the MPLSS and the CPLSS, the pri-
mary heat rejection component is a radia-
tor,4,5 which has an aluminum plate cover-
ing the back of the PLSS backpack on the
astronaut (Figure 7). A narrow gas gap
separates this exterior aluminum panel
from the water panel. The water panel is
the structure that the water diverted from
the WCL flows through.
Unlike the shuttle design, the radiator
requires no consumables or the presence
of a vacuum. However, the environmen-
tal or effective temperature must be much
less than the radiator temperature for it to
reject appreciable amounts of heat. So, in
a hot environment, this actually will heat
the water significantly. For this reason,
both PLSS designs use secondary cool-
ing devices for use in hot environments.
On Mars, the added effect of convec-
tion over the flat plate of the radiator
would help to increase its performance,
while any buildup of dust will decrease
its performance. Simulation models of the
two new PLSS designs have been devel-
oped6,7 to assist in the design of the com-
the water used by the WB comes from the
WCL, which has some extra water stored
in an accumulator, so there are no
feedwater tanks in the design.
The Trim Cooler4 (TC) is a simple heat
exchanger that has the LCG water in one
coil and the vent loop gas in another. The
colder WCL water removes heat from the
higher temperature vent gas. The WB’s
overall heat transfer coefficient (UA) is
approximately 14.56 Btu/h·F (7.68 W/K)
and the TC’s UA is 5.1 Btu·F (0.67 W/K). A
higher UA value helps keep the size small.
The secondary heat rejection compo-
nent of the CPLSS is the Liquid Oxygen
heat eXchanger (LOX).4 The CPLSS, dis-
cussed in more detail later, uses liquid

water temperature can be found by:
QH2O = mO2 (hfO2i – hgO2) + mH2O CP,H2O (TH2Oi – TH2Oo) (6)
where,
mH2O
= Mass flow rate of the water.
CPH2O
= Specific heat of the water.
TH2O
= Temperature of the exiting water.
TH2Oi
= Temperature of the inlet water.
mO2
= Mass flow rate of the oxygen.
hO2fi
= Enthalpy of the entering liquid oxygen.
hgO2
= Enthalpy of the saturated oxygen vapor at
the exit.
For the next two stages, the following equations are used to
determine the temperatures of the exiting water, the exiting oxy-
gen gas, and the temperatures of the two tubes that they flow in.
QΗ2Ο = mH2O CP,Η2Ο (Τin,H2O – Tout,H2O) + UA (Tin,O2 – Tout,H2O)
QO2 = mO2 CP,O2 (Tin,O2 – Tout,O2) + UA (Tin,H2O – Tout,O2) (7– 8)
where, the UA is the overall heat transfer coefficient between
the two fluids.
Heating. The Apollo, the DMA, and the shuttle PLSS have
no active heating for the WCL or the VL. The only heating
elements in the shuttle PLSS are in the gloves. The MPLSS and
CPLSS remedy this situation and will be equipped with comfort
oxygen as the breathing gas supply. This oxygen must be heated
to a breathable temperature and this is done using the LOX.
The LOX is a simple concentric tube heat exchanger that has in
the inner tube the LCG water and in the outer tube the liquid
oxygen. The Cooling Control Valve (CCV) controls the amount
of oxygen flowing through the LOX and in effect this controls
the amount of secondary heat rejection. The maximum and mini-
mum flow rates of the oxygen are 5 lbm/h (2.27 kg/h) and 0.79
lbm/h (0.385 kg/h) respectively. The mass flow rate of the water
is expected to be 200 lbm/h (90.71 kg/h). The total heat transfer
required to heat and vaporize the liquid oxygen to a breathable
temperature (assumed 70°F [21.1°C]) is given as:
qO2 = mO2 (hO2 – hiO2)
(5)
where,
mO2
= the mass flow rate of the oxygen.
hO2
= the enthalpy of gaseous oxygen at 70°F
(21.1°C).
hiO2
= the enthalpy of liquid oxygen at –297°F
(–183°C).
The maximum and minimum heat transfer rates that can be
expected are 958.61 Btu/h (1011.4 kJ/h) and 162.57 Btu/h (171.15
kJ/h) which is a significant amount of the cooling required for
the astronaut. These heat transfer rates will result in LCG water
temperature ∆ Ts of 4.8°F (2.66°C) and 0.814°F (0.451°C). The

heaters in the WCL. 4
These heaters are simple
electrical elements that can
provide up to 50 W of
heating to the WCL.
Pumping. All three U.S.
designs use a constant
volume pump to circulate
the water through the loop
while the DMA2 uses a
variable flow rate pump.
The pump in the Apollo
suit7 supplied water at 240
lbm/h (109 kg/h). The
DMA flow rate range is
198 to 396 lbm/h (89.8 to
179.6 kg/h). The Shuttle
PLSS3 uses a fan-pump-
separator combination for both the vent
loop and water loop and water flow is a
constant 240 lbm/h (109 kg/h). The
MPLSS and CPLSS use a dedicated pump
to maintain the water flow rate at 200 lbm/h
(90.71 kg/h).
A summary of the cooling and heating
components of the five designs can be
found in Table 2. This table shows the
capabilities of the three existing designs
in space and the MPLSS and the CPLSS
in the typical Martian environment men-
tioned earlier at the average metabolic rate
of 275 W. The radiator does not have the
heat rejection capability of the sublimator.
However, the radiator is a far more reliable
design that significantly reduces consum-
able usage and can be used in space or on
