April 2001
ASHRAE Journal
4 7
ASHRAE Journal
Gas Cooling
I
Advances in Natural
Gas Cooling
About the Authors
By Gary A. Nowakowski and Rebecca L. Busby
Member ASHRAE
n addition to absorption chillers, today’s natural gas cooling technologies
include engine-driven systems in a range of capacities and temperature
capabilities suitable for most commercial air-conditioning and refrigera-
tion applications. This article reviews the history of natural gas cooling, de-
scribes recent advances in natural gas cooling technology, and character-
izes the commercial cooling market and applications.
Historical Perspective
Mechanical refrigeration systems date
back to the mid-1800s. By 1889, central-
station refrigeration was used in large cit-
ies to preserve foods and documents. Air-
conditioning technology was pioneered
by Willis Carrier, who patented his “ap-
paratus for treating air” in 1906. The term
“air conditioning” was also coined that
year by engineer Stuart W. Cramer. The
first air-conditioning applications in-
cluded printing companies and textile
mills, which required humidity control.
In the early 1900s, Alfred Wolff de-
signed and installed comfort cooling sys-
tems in the New York area, including the
Stock Exchange building. Air-condition-
ing systems of various types were soon
applied to movie theaters.
Carrier patented the centrifugal refrig-
eration machine for air conditioning large
spaces in 1921. Theaters and factories
continued to be the most important mar-
kets for the budding air-conditioning in-
dustry through 1930.
During the 1930s and ’40s, cooling tech-
nology expanded into the residential mar-
ket with the development and mass pro-
duction of home refrigerators and unitary
air conditioners. In 1946, 30,000 room air
conditioners were shipped, and comfort
cooling began to move into office build-
ings and other commercial workplaces.
The natural gas industry launched re-
search on natural gas-fueled air condi-
tioning in 1941, resulting in development
of a lithium bromide-water unit designed
for residential applications.
During the 1950s, several U.S. manu-
facturers of HVAC equipment developed
and introduced large-tonnage lithium
bromide-water absorption chillers. By the
1960s, about 40% of all large-tonnage
chillers delivered worldwide were absorp-
tion machines. Domestic sales totaled
more than 500,000 tons (1.8 million kW)
in 1968. Most of this era’s units were pow-
ered by steam from natural gas-fired boil-
ers or supplied by utilities. In addition,
some manufacturers sold mechanical re-
frigeration systems with compressors
powered by industrial engines.
However, governmental control of
natural gas prices, which originated with
the 1938 Natural Gas Act, was extended
by a 1954 U.S. Supreme Court ruling that
prices at the wellhead (where natural gas
is produced) could also be regulated.
These price controls proved to be a dis-
incentive for natural gas producers, who
found that exploration for oil, particularly
in foreign countries, was far more lucra-
tive than developing natural gas reserves
in the U.S. As a result, during the 1960s
and early ’70s, natural gas drilling activ-
ity slowed, supplies dwindled, and some
large natural gas customers were curtailed.
Because of natural gas supply uncer-
tainty Arising from price regulation, sales
of absorption chillers dropped sharply in
the late 1960s and continued to decline
through the 1970s and early ’80s. Cool-
ing equipment manufacturers focused on
improving electric-powered technology,
which dominated the market. Beginning
in the 1960s, Japanese HVAC companies
continued to advance absorption tech-
nology, including the development of di-
rect-fired double-effect machines.
Since the 1980s, due to deregulation of
the U.S. natural gas industry, natural gas
supplies have increased while prices
have declined. Although natural gas dis-
tribution companies are still regulated by
state and local governments, the trans-
portation of natural gas by pipeline was
gradually deregulated during 1985–93,
allowing customers to buy natural gas
directly from producers, and wellhead
prices were decontrolled from 1989 to
1993, allowing the wholesale price to be
set freely in the marketplace.
The natural gas industry resumed de-
velopment of natural gas cooling tech-
nology during the 1980s (see Recent Tech-
nology Developments later). U.S. compa-
nies acquired the rights to double-effect
chiller/heater technologies from Japanese
sources, adapted the technologies for
U.S. requirements, and began manufac-
Gary A. Nowakowski is team leader,
Market Opportunities Team, U.S. De-
partment of Energy, Chicago Regional
Office. Previously, he was principal
product manager with the Gas Re-
search Institute (GRI), Chicago.
Rebecca L. Busby is a freelance
technical writer/editor. Formerly, she
was editor of GRI’s magazine, GRID.
4 8
ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
April 2001
ASHRAE Journal
turing units in North America in the early 1990s. Engine-driven
product development was also initiated by several manufactur-
ers.
Domestic absorption chiller shipments reached approximately
200,000 refrigeration tons (700 000 kW) in 1994 and 1995 (Fig-
ure 1). Also during the 1990s, engine-driven system sales in-
creased about ten-fold, growing from less than 5,000 tons (17
600 kW) in 1993 to more than 50,000 tons (176 000 kW) in 1998–
99 (Figure 2). Natural gas-powered chillers now account for
nearly 8% of large-tonnage, water-cooled chillers sold in the
U.S., up from 4% in 1990.
Recent Technology Developments
Since the absorption chiller sales peak of the late 1960s, both
absorption and engine-driven natural gas cooling technolo-
gies have been advanced in efficiency and performance by U.S.
HVAC manufacturers. GRI, the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE),
and many natural gas companies have participated in many of
these developments and continue to support ongoing research.
Absorption Technology
Absorption chillers can be fired directly with natural gas or
other fuel, or they can be fired indirectly with steam from natu-
ral gas boilers or central systems. Absorption chillers tradition-
ally have been recognized for quiet, vibration-free operation
and low emissions. Also, they use water instead of chlorofluo-
rocarbons as the refrigerant, giving absorption technology an
environmental advantage.
Significant advances in energy efficiency were achieved dur-
ing the 1960s with the introduction of double-effect absorption
cycles, which increase chiller coefficient of performance (COP)
to about 1.0 from previous single-effect COPs of around 0.7.
Currently, DOE is cofunding development of a triple-effect de-
sign with a major U.S. HVAC manufacturer. Prototype testing has
confirmed the potential to reach COPs of 1.4 or greater. A 450-ton
(1580-kW) field demonstration unit was scheduled to begin op-
eration in late 2000 at a government facility in Las Vegas.
Despite these advances, the application of absorption chill-
ers has been limited by their larger size and higher first cost,
compared with electric units. A research consortium is funding
work at the University of Maryland on the basic technology
needed to make absorption chillers less expensive and more
compact. These efforts include:
•
Computerized modeling to optimize the design of absorber
tube bundles,
•
Software for the thermodynamic properties of aqueous
lithium bromide solutions in the concentration and temperature
ranges relevant to absorption chiller design,
•
Enhancing internal and external geometry to improve heat
and mass transfer rates, and
•
Developing new heat and mass transfer additives for
lithium bromide solutions.
Another team is investigating the use of a proprietary pro-
cess that could allow absorption chillers to be made out of less
expensive materials, as well as prolong chiller life.
Several recent improvements have reduced absorption chiller
maintenance cost to the realm of equivalency with electric units.
The vacuum required for the absorption cooling process is
preserved by better welding techniques, higher quality materi-
als, and designs that minimize shell penetrations and leaks.
While early units required manual purging of noncondensables
to prevent corrosion, modern absorption machines incorporate
automatic purge systems that remove air as soon as it is de-
tected by the microprocessor controls. In a recently introduced
chiller/heater, the automatic purge pump and vacuum gauges
are no longer required, due to the system’s vacuum integrity.
Also, variable-speed pumps have been incorporated in natural
gas absorption chillers to improve setpoint control.
Electronic controls have largely eliminated accidental crystal-
lization of working fluids in absorption chillers, which has caused
operating problems in the past. Advanced control systems can
maintain precise concentrations of lithium bromide in absorption
machines. However, additives that actively inhibit crystallization
could further improve absorption chiller performance. Research
projects at the Arizona State University and the University of
Figure 1: Absorption chiller shipments.
Figure 2: Engine chiller shipments.
Figure 3: Average commercial electricity rates.
April 2001
ASHRAE Journal
4 9
Gas Cooling
Utah studied the effects of various inorganic and organic addi-
tives that can lower crystallization temperature by as much as
72°F (40°C). One of these inhibitors is currently being tested.
Engine Driven Technology
Cooling systems driven by natural gas engines are recog-
nized for high-efficiency, variable-speed operation, and heat re-
covery capabilities. Full-load COPs for today’s engine-driven
chillers range from 1.0 for machines with
reciprocating compressors, 1.3 to 1.9 for
screw compressor chillers, and 1.9 for
units with centrifugal compressors. Be-
cause variable-speed operation of en-
gine-driven systems increases their ef-
ficiency at part load, engine chiller effi-
ciency can be represented more accu-
rately by the integrated part load value
(IPLV), a weighted average seasonal ef-
ficiency as defined by the American Re-
frigeration Institute (ARI Standard 550/
590-98). The IPLV provides a standard
basis upon which chiller efficiencies can
be compared on a duty cycle more typi-
cal of field installations. Typical water-
cooled engine chillers reach an IPLV of
1.9 to 2.5 for screw compressor units
and 2.5 for centrifugal compressor ma-
chines.
The efficiency of engine-driven sys-
tems is increased by 15 to 25% by re-
covering heat from the engine’s jacket
coolant (radiator fluid) and exhaust gas.
With heat recovery equipment, engine-
driven systems can convert up to 75%
of their fuel input to useful heat and
shaft power.
Despite these advantages, the appli-
cation of engine-driven natural gas
cooling systems has been limited by air
quality regulations, maintenance and
noise concerns, the larger physical size
of early engine systems compared with
electric units, and the lack of integra-
tion between the engine and refrigera-
tion subsystems. During the 1980s to
90s, technology development focused
on integration of electronic control sys-
tems, reduction of maintenance require-
ments, emissions control, more compact
designs, noise abatement, and basic
engine improvements.
Engine chiller control systems main-
tain the desired chilled water setpoint
by varying the engine speed and com-
pressor loading, resulting in turndown
ratios of 10:1 or greater. These controls
integrate the engine and refrigeration
subsystems and control the complex interactions between them.
Electronic control systems also assist in diagnosing operational
problems by detecting out-of-range temperatures, pressures,
and flow rates.
Onboard diagnostics and remote monitoring of key operat-
ing parameters have simplified and reduced maintenance and
associated costs. Using a dedicated telephone line, remote moni-
toring and communication systems can perform the following
Advertisement in print edition formerly in this space.
5 0
ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
April 2001
ASHRAE Journal
functions:
•
Start and stop the chiller, change the chilled-wa-
ter setpoint, and adjust the engine’s maximum speed
setting
•
Review the real-time status of the unit, the alarm
history, and the load profile,
•
Retrieve historical data, including data recorded
during the minutes preceding an alarm, and
•
Clear an alarm or pre-alarm.
Advancements in engine technologies have also
resulted in reduced maintenance, as well as greater
efficiency, lower emissions, and longer life, largely due
to technological improvements in engine design and
manufacturing by the automotive industry and indus-
trial engine manufacturers. Scheduled maintenance of
engine-driven machines has become a relatively minor
operating cost factor (approximately 2¢/ton-hr, or 0.6¢/
kW-hr). Automotive derivative engines have been de-
veloped with a useful life of 20,000 hours or more, re-
sulting in products with more competitive first costs.
Industrial (diesel derivative) engines generally require
a top-end overhaul (rebuild or exchange the cylinder
head assembly) at 12,000 to 15,000 hours of operation and a
total overhaul (replace crankshaft bearings, seals, and cylinder
head) at 24,000 to 30,000 hours.
“Lean burn” technology (which uses excess oxygen in the
fuel/air mix) results in more complete combustion of the fuel
and hence lower carbon monoxide levels and higher efficiency.
Excess air also reduces combustion temperatures, which de-
creases emissions of nitrogen oxides (NOx). Most lean burn
engines are turbocharged to boost horsepower and efficiency.
Engine chillers and refrigeration systems can require an emis-
sions operating permit in cities with air quality problems. Manu-
facturers offer emission control equipment using three-way
catalytic after-treatment of exhaust gas or other control tech-
nologies. Engine-driven systems meet strict regulations in
southern California by using stoichiometric combustion, emis-
sion controls, and three-way catalyst exhaust after-treatment.
In most other regions, a lean burn engine can meet local emis-
sion regulations without exhaust after-treatment.
GRI conducted a field test of engine chiller catalytic emission
control technology during 1997. The control system, using an
oxygen feedback sensor, adjusts the engine’s fuel/air ratio for
optimal catalyst performance. Test data demonstrated that emis-
sion controls using a three-way catalyst were capable of reduc-
ing NOx below 0.5 grams per brake horsepower-hour (g/bhp-
hr), carbon monoxide below 1.0 g/bhp-hr, and non-methane hy-
drocarbons below 1.5 g/bhp-hr.
The physical size of engine-driven chillers and refrigeration
systems has been reduced by design and packaging, and manu-
facturers offer some equipment with the same square footage as
electric units. One common design is to locate the engine/com-
pressor driveline above the evaporator/condenser heat exchang-
ers. With a sound attenuation enclosure, engine noise is equiva-
lent to an electric machine and, at part load, can be quieter.
In contrast to older engine-driven cooling systems, today’s
units are offered as standard products rather than being cus-
tom designed, resulting in lower costs and shorter lead times.
Standardization was first applied to 150-ton (527-kW) engine
chiller systems in the late 1980s. Since then, the capacity of
these products has been increased to 1,000 tons (3500 kW).
Recently, a hybrid chiller was introduced which incorporates
both engine and motor drive, allowing the system to be pow-
ered by either natural gas or electricity. This unit offers reliabil-
ity and flexibility of energy choice to operators who need only
one chiller or have limited room for equipment. In research
funded by DOE’s Fossil Energy Technology Center, an ad-
vanced version is being developed to generate electricity and
produce chilled water simultaneously. The motor will also func-
tion as a synchronous generator to provide emergency power.
Chiller Market Perspective
The U.S. space cooling market is dominated by a few large
manufacturers, but many smaller players also produce, pack-
age, and sell chillers, chiller/heaters, and unitary air condition-
ers. Three types of commercial natural gas cooling systems are
currently available in the U.S.:
• Absorption chillers. All major HVAC equipment manufac-
turers offer large-tonnage natural gas absorption systems, in-
cluding direct-fired single-effect chillers and direct- and indi-
rect-fired double-effect machines. For the light commercial mar-
ket, small-tonnage water-cooled absorption chillers (<100 tons
or 350 kW) are available, as well as unitary absorption equip-
ment in sizes as low as 3 to 5 tons (11 to 17 kW).
• Engine-driven chillers. Five manufacturers participate in the
natural gas engine chiller market, offering both air-cooled and
water-cooled products. Two manufacturers provide units in the
10 to 50 ton (35 to 176 kW) size range for light commercial appli-
cations, while maximum sizes range up to 2,000 tons (7034 kW).
• Engine-driven refrigeration systems. Four manufacturers
dominate the market for commercial refrigeration equipment;
one of these offers a standard natural gas-engine-driven prod-

5 1
uct line, while the other three offer cus-
tom-built engine refrigeration systems.
A recent GRI analysis projects that natu-
ral gas consumed by commercial space
cooling systems in the U.S. could increase
from 123 trillion Btu in 1998 to as much as
300 trillion Btu in 2010, accounting for an
estimated 7% of natural gas used in com-
mercial applications. Market drivers for this
increase in natural gas cooling include
growth in commercial floor space, growth
in energy-intensive applications, and the
lower life-cycle costs of natural gas sys-
tems compared with electric equipment.
Chiller Economics and Applications
Both absorption and engine-driven
natural gas cooling systems cost more to
purchase than comparable electric equip-
ment. The primary market driver for natu-
ral gas chillers is the operating cost sav-
ings achievable in areas with relatively
high electric rates, which are shown in
Figure 3. Approximately 25% of commer-
cial customers in the U.S. do business in
these high electric rate areas, where op-
erating cost savings can produce a re-
turn of 20% or more on the equipment’s
incremental first cost premium over com-
peting conventional chillers. The savings
result from a combination of lower energy
and demand charges.
In general, applications that are best
suited for natural gas cooling are energy-
intensive (high consumption per square
foot) and/or require operation during peak
periods. Coincident demand for heat can
also be an important factor in cooling sys-
tem economics because heat can be re-
covered from natural gas engine-driven
chillers at temperatures high enough to
produce hot water at 180°F to 230°F (82°C
to 110°C) or, in some cases, low-pressure
steam (15 psig [205 kPa]).
Natural gas cooling products are suit-
able for applications in which one or more
of these factors prevail:
• The building or facility is being ex-
panded, requiring the installation of ad-
ditional electric capacity for air condition-
ing or refrigeration.
• Air-conditioning blackout protection
is needed. This is often required for health-
care facilities such as hospitals and nurs-
ing homes. In this case, a natural gas chiller
can be combined with a smaller generator
set than required by an electric chiller,
which reduces the natural gas system’s
first-cost premium.
•
The building or process has a coin-
cident demand for heat and air condition-
ing. In this case, engine chiller heat can be
Advertisement in print edition formerly in this space.
Advertisement in print edition formerly
in this space.
5 2
ASHRAE Journal
April 2001
directly used, thus increasing operating savings and improving
economics. Common uses for the engine heat include the pre-
heat of boiler feedwater, swimming pools, laundries, dishwashing,
desiccant regeneration, and hot water for clean-up purposes.
•
The business is located in an area with high electric de-
mand charges and energy rates, where the operating cost sav-
ings can be great enough to pay back the first-cost premium
typically in three to five years.
•
The building or facility is owner-occupied, providing in-
centive for energy cost savings, and air conditioning or refrig-
eration represents a significant business cost.
Applications for natural gas cooling based on these factors
include hospitals and other health-care facilities (see Table 1),
colleges and universities, and hotels/motels. Public-use facili-
ties that incur high peak demand charges, such as ice arenas,
stadiums, and convention centers, are also good candidates.
Government facilities can be included as possible applica-
tions for natural gas cooling because the federal government
recognizes the value of “source” efficiency, defined as the overall
utilization of energy not only at the user’s site, but also during
production and transport from the source to the user. Executive
Order 13213 issued in June 1999 requires federal agencies to
evaluate projects based on consumption of source energy rather
than site energy. Evaluated by this measure, the efficiency of
natural gas cooling systems is roughly comparable to electric
systems or, in the case of engine-driven chillers with heat re-
covery, 20% to 40% higher.
In the refrigeration business, natural gas engine-driven sys-
tems have found early application in wineries, ice arenas, ice-
making, meat processing, and refrigerated warehouses. Com-
mercial natural gas-powered refrigeration could be economical
in other small food processing applications.
In the power generation market, absorption and engine-driven
chillers can be used to cool the air going into natural gas tur-
bines to about 50°F (10°C). Without inlet air cooling, turbines
lose significant capacity during hot weather, when the electric-
ity is most needed. At a turbine cogeneration facility in Lathorp,
Calif., inlet air cooling with a 1,400-ton (4900-kW) absorption
machine allowed the plant to generate 36% more power. The
absorption unit was fired with low-pressure steam from the
plant’s waste heat boiler.

Power Up
Standard 90.1-1999
Energy Standard for Buildings Except
Low-Rise Residential Buildings
Jointly sponsored by ASHRAE and IESNA, and
approved by ANSI, the latest edition of this
popular standard contains numerous improve-
ments over the 1989 version. Examples include
enhancements in energy efficiency levels, an
expanded scope to include existing buildings, and
mandatory enforceable language suitable for
code adoption. The standard, now available in
separate SI and I-P editions, addresses both
national and international needs. Other improve-
ments include the simplification of energy effi-
ciency requirements and prescriptive options for
all components, in addition to performance-
based approaches.

Thermal Storage
C
About the Authors
By Qingqin Wang and Ximei Zhao
hina’s energy industry has been developing rapidly, especially the
power industry. The power generation capacity has increased by
about 12 million kW annually. The total capacity of installed elec-
tric power generators in mainland China has topped 300 million kW, and
China ranks second in the world for installed capacity.
thermal energy storage air-conditioning
systems. If that is done, it is estimated
that 15 to 25 billion RMB ($1.8 to $3 bil-
lion U.S.) would be saved, and related
enterprises would benefit from operating
cost savings.
Background
During the 1970s, cold-water storage
technology was used in gymnasiums, but
was not widely adopted. In the early
1990s, ice thermal energy storage air-
conditioning systems began to be used.
By the end of 1998, more than 100
TES projects were in existence (see Tables
1 and 2). In addition, nearly 20
companies and factories produced TES
equipment and TES research in main-
land China.
The First Three TES projects
In the early 1990s, three TES projects
began operation (two ice storage and one
cold water storage). They are:
Shenzhen Electronic Science and
Technology Building: Located in
Shenzhen City, Guangdong province in
southern China, this was the first ice stor-
age air-conditioning system installed in
mainland China. This project included a
floor area of 62 000 m2 (667,400 ft2), and a
calculated cooling design day load of
Thermal Energy
Storage in China
However, the power industry still can-
not meet the increasing needs of indus-
trial, commercial and residential sectors
in China. Power is still in short supply,
particularly in coastal and economically
developed areas. For example, the power
supply in north China, east China, and
south China was tight in the summer 2000.
On June 5 in Guangdong Province, all cit-
ies except Shaoguan District had a lim-
ited power supply. On this day, 436 power
lines and about 1.3 million kW of electric-
ity were switched off.
One reason for the power shortage
is that the load ratio of the power net-
work is very low, while the peak and off-
peak load difference is very high. This
causes a shortage of peak power and
much of the off-peak power is wasted. In
recent years, the average load ratio of
China’s power network has been decreas-
ing by 1% annually. The off-peak power
load accounts for only 70% to 75% of the
peak load.
To shift peak load to off-peak time, the
National Planning Commission, Economy
and Trade Commission, Ministry of Elec-
trical Power issued Circular 703 in early
1994. The circular planned for 10 to 12
million kW of peak power load to be
shifted to off-peak time in five years. Three
to 5 million kW needed to be shifted by
11,250 kW. The ice-storage capacity was
24,190 kWh. Ice balls were used.
The Comprehensive Office Building
of Beijing Daily: The floor area of this
building is 15 200 m2 (163,610 ft2). Calcu-
lated cooling load was 1,512 kW. Ice balls
were used. The project included three hori-
zontal ice storage tanks (2.4 m in diameter
and 6 m in length [8 ft by 20 ft]).
Qingyuan Xinbeijiang Drug Co.: Lo-
cated in Qingyuan city of Guangdong
province, this TES system is for
production use. It is a cold-water storage
system with a design cooling load of 1744
kW. The cold water tank was 1083 m3
(286,100 gal) and covered an area of
110 m2 (1184 ft2). The cold storage
density was 21.4 kW/m3, and the cold
storage capacity was 232 000 kW
(65,965 tons).
Most of the glycol chillers used in TES
systems in China were imported from the
United States or Europe. Less than 10
TES projects adopted chillers made by
other companies.
TES Policies
The rapid development of China’s na-
tional economy resulted in a swift rise of
power consumption, especially in the
coastal and economically developed ar-
eas. As stated previously, it had been
decided to promote the peak and off-peak
rate policy to alleviate the peak power
Qingqin Wang is deputy director and
senior engineer at the Institute of Air
Conditioning, China Academy of
Building Research, Beijing, China.
Ximei Zhao is a lecturer at the
Department of Physics, Xingtai
Normal College in Xingtai City, Hebei
Province, China.
5 4
ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
April 2001
ASHRAE Journal
shortage. The North China (Huabei)
Power Group was the first to issue its
preferential policy that the ratio of peak
to off-peak power rate was 4.5. Later,
other local electric power companies and
bureaus issued many preferential
policies.
In 1997, the Power Bureau of Hangzhou
City, Zhejiang Province, published a no-
tice that states the following policies:
When the cold storage capacity of a
TES system is more than 500 refrigera-
tion ton hours (1758 kW) and the shifted
power is more than 28% of the peak load,
the central air-conditioning system user
would be awarded with a power index of
100,000 kWh, and:
• Central air-conditioning system us-
ers would be charged according to peak
and off-peak power rate, which saves on
operating costs.
• Air-conditioning equipment may run
during a peak power period. Because of
peak power shortage, many other cen-
tral air-conditioning systems are not al-
lowed to run during the peak time.
• For some special TES demonstra-
tion projects, the electrical demand
charge of 30% to 50% of chiller capacity
will be exempted according to the con-
crete conditions.
Because of the preferential policy, TES
system and equipment in Hangzhou have
developed rapidly.
In April 1995, the National Center for
Cold Storage Air Conditioning Research
was established, which is subordinate to
the Chinese Association of Energy
Efficiency. The main purpose of this cen-
ter is to promote the study of TES tech-
nology, the development of TES equip-
ment and the application of the TES sys-
tem. In January 1999, the center was re-
named the TES Air Conditioning Commit-
tee of the Chinese Association of Energy
Efficiency.
In April 1998, the National Electrical Co.
published, “Promoting the Application of
TES-type Electric Boilers and Making Use
of the Off-Peak Electricity.”
To promote the application of TES tech-
nology, China Economy and Trade Com-
mittee issued Circular 298[1997]. In this
circular, TES air-conditioning systems are
key to future development.
At the end of 1998, the State Council
issued Circular 32[1998], and North China
Power Group issued Circular 30[1998],
both circulars emphasized promoting TES
systems and encouraging the use of off-
peak electricity.
The ratio of peak to off-peak rate and
the peak load period are listed in Table 3.
Developing TES
Preferential policies are needed to pro-
mote the widespread use of TES systems.
Local governments should award the us-
ers who adopt TES technology to shift
peak power load to off-peak time. The pref-
erential policies should benefit power
companies, TES investors and users.
It is necessary to import, assimilate and
absorb new and advanced technologies
from abroad. We may first cooperate with
TES manufacturers and companies, and

set up some demonstration projects.
For the advanced technologies suited
to China, we should import or set up
joint ventures.
All types of TES systems should be
studied and developed for different ap-
plications. The new TES system should
shift peak load to off-peak time and re-
duce energy consumption. This would
make it easy to extend a TES system.
Air-cooled heat pump chillers are used
in many small and medium buildings.
There is a need to develop and install TES-
type heat pump installations to shift loads
and save power.
Phase Change Materials (PCMs) are ex-
cellent media for storing energy. They
have many advantages, including that
low-temperature chillers are not needed,
high-chiller efficiency, and glycol
solutions are not needed, they are suit-
able for retrofitted projects, and they are
easy to design and install. For these
reasons, it is necessary to develop new
high quality eutectic salts and large
storage capacity.
The cold air supply system has the ben-
efits of low investment, the reduction of
peak electrical consumption and running
cost, and the savings of building area and
space. However, the design method and
equipment manufacturing need to be
studied and developed. The equipment
related to cold air-distribution system in-
April 2001
ASHRAE Journal
5 5
cludes air-handling units, air supply out-
lets, heat exchangers, air terminals, etc.
Codes and standards related to cold air
system need to be compiled.
TES technology is unfamiliar for most
engineers in China, so codes and techni-
cal measures for the design, installation,
commissioning, maintenance of TES sys-
tems, and to strengthen the related tech-
nical exchange and training are needed.
The Future of TES
During 1999 and 2000, it is estimated that
more than 60 new TES projects were built.
Only the Beijing area has had more than
20 new TES projects (including hot water
storage systems for building heating).
Recently in Beijing, the rate of off-peak
time electricity was cut down from 0.19
RMB/kWh to 0.16 RMB/kWh (about $0.02
U.S.). In Shanghai, the peak and off-peak
power rate policy was put into effect. It is
certain that more preferential policies will
be carried out for the TES system.
TES technology has a bright future in
China and much work needs to be done.
We hope to set up good relations with
research institutes, manufacturers and
companies worldwide to extend the ap-
plication of TES technology.
Bibliography

power supply shortage stated by electric ex-
perts.” Economy Daily, August 6.

and technologies for ice thermal energy stor-
age air conditioning systems.” Report of Na-
tional Key Research Project during the Ninth
Five-year Plan Period. October.

ing, Refrigeration and Air Conditioning, No.3.

ogy of Cold Storage for Air Conditioning,

cold water storage for air conditioning sys-
tems.” Huazhong HV&AC, No.1.

conditioning system.” 1998. Refrigeration and

ASHRAE Journal

40
ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
May 2001
W
The HVAC Response
To the Energy Challenge
About the Authors
By Jay Althof,
Eugene Smithart and Jane Sidebottom
Associate Member ASHRAE
Member ASHRAE
ith the media coverage of the energy situation in California, it
would be difficult not to be concerned about the status of energy
availability and pricing throughout the U.S. Last year, more than
$220 billion was spent in the U.S. to heat, cool and light buildings.1 With
some geographic regions experiencing significant increases in electricity and
natural gas prices, this value could be much higher in the years to come.
productivity levels. But to do so, we must
understand the fundamental drivers be-
hind the issues and deliver sustainable
solutions to business owners who are
directly affected by these challenges.
Short-Term Crisis or Long-Term
Challenge?
One of the first issues to consider is
whether the current situation in Califor-
nia is an isolated challenge or more wide-
spread. To gain a clearer understanding
of the situation, it is valuable to look at a
few of the fundamental drivers that have
resulted in the California crisis and then
compare these drivers with other areas of
the country. The three major physical as-
pects of reliable electric power are gen-
eration, transmission, and distribution.
In California, a shortage exists in gen-
eration capacity. The reasons are not
unique to California. As many are already
aware, deregulation of the electric power
industry has initiated the demolition of the
traditional utility business model where
utility rates were set based on a return on
assets. Anticipating this, many utilities
around the country avoided building new
energy assets, power plants, as it was un-
clear if in the emerging deregulated market
they could achieve an acceptable return
on their investments. Additionally, stud-
ies indicated that sufficient supply would
be available to meet demand.
However, growth rates in several parts
of the country have exceeded expecta-
tion — particularly in the Silicon Valley
region of California where analysts have
been proven incorrect.
Take, for instance, the demand for the
computer systems powering the “infor-
mation age.” Consumption in this area
alone has grown to about 74 tWh (tera
watt-hours) per year, or roughly 2% of the
overall U.S. electricity consumption.2 Al-
though this is a relatively small increase,
it is additive to the already growing need
for electricity. Sharp demand combined
with stricter environmental regulation has
made it difficult for companies willing to
invest in cogeneration or merchant plants
to obtain necessary permits. Similar situa-
tions exist in other areas of the country
including the Southwest—notably
Texas—the Midwest, Mid-Atlantic region
and New England. All areas of the coun-
try have experienced shortages due to
unexpected temperature variances, storm
damage or maintenance problems.
Uncertainties about the availability and
reliability of electricity are not confined to
just the plant. In some parts of the United
States, utilities have been reluctant to in-
vest in the transmission system that, like
other aspects of America’s infrastructure,
is aging and subject to failure. Further,
transmission and physical distribution
systems were designed to meet customer
demand within a utility’s assigned terri-
tory. Aging systems and lack of invest-
Jay Althof is vice president and gen-
eral manager, Trane Building Automa-
tion Systems.
Eugene Smithart is director of envi-
ronmental affairs, The Trane Company.
Jane Sidebottom is director of mar-
ket development, The Trane Company.
Many in the HVAC industry remember,
or perhaps started their careers as a re-
sult of the energy crisis of the mid to late
1970s. We have learned much since then
about how to better manage and conserve
energy. Many of the techniques used 20
years ago have been refined and signifi-
cant advances in technology have been
made, which has resulted in more efficient
systems and components.
Now the industry needs to take a lead-
ing role in serving our customers and by
finding solutions to this latest energy
challenge. HVAC is one of the major en-
ergy consumers in buildings. People look
to our industry for answers during times
of crisis. President Jimmy Carter’s famous
address urging Americans to turn down
their thermostats, delivered while wear-
ing a sweater, is an image that remains in
the minds of many who experienced the
last energy crisis. However, it is not often
a wise economic decision to sacrifice pro-
ductivity, due to a less comfortable work-
ing environment, for the energy savings
that result from “brute force” conserva-
tion measures.
Not only do we have the responsibility
to respond to this energy challenge, we
have the opportunity to regain some of
the respect the HVAC industry deserves
by delivering solid energy savings and
helping business owners maintain high
May 2001
ASHRAE Journal
41
Energy
ment has made it difficult to transport
power reliably. Further concern or confu-
sion surrounding distribution in a deregu-
lated market makes the question of avail-
ability and reliability of electricity an enor-
mous uncertainty to customers.
The U.S. Department of Energy, along
with the private sector, continues to in-
vest in finding alternative means of elec-
tricity. Investment continues in the search
of finding commercially viable sources of
renewable energy including photovolta-
ics and mini-hydro-based forms of gen-
eration. Incentives have primarily been
directed toward residential application and
remain a challenge for commercial and in-
dustrial use. Newer technologies, prima-
rily micro-turbines and fuel cells, have
been under development for several years
and are being used commercially to pro-
vide primary and secondary sources of
electricity.
Our conclusion is that while the en-
ergy situation may not be as dramatic as
it was in the late 1970s, it clearly is and
will be a challenge—not only in Califor-
nia but also throughout the United
States. The difference is that most the
country has a few years to prepare for
the coming shortages in capacity by re-
ducing demand and consumption and se-
lectively building additional generation
and transmission capacity.
Lessons Learned
In the late 1970s and early 1980s, HVAC
and building control professionals used
several strategies to respond to the en-
ergy crisis. Table 1 outlines some of the
more common measures of that time. This
crisis was the key factor in the rise of
energy management systems (EMS),
which have since expanded in scope and
have become more commonly known as
building automation systems (BAS).
Since then, the HVAC and building con-
trols industry has developed many addi-
tional product and system strategies that
can be implemented to meet our current
challenges. Table 2 outlines several of
these current options.
Clearly, building professionals have
many conservation strategies at their dis-
posal to reduce energy demand and con-
sumption through the proper use of
these methods. However, even simple
and cost-effective options such as re-
placing lower efficiency T12 fluorescent
bulbs with higher efficiency T8 lamps,
are estimated to have been done in only
about 30% of buildings in the United
Table 1:
1970s energy
conservation
measures
Table 2:
2001 energy
conservation
measures.
•
Duty cycling
•
Introduction of VAV systems
•
0.8 or 0.9 kW/Ton chillers and equivalent efficiencies on other
products
•
Initial use of energy analysis tools such as TRACE™, BLAST and
DOE
•
Custom-designed thermal energy storage systems
•
Physically limit the amount of outside air
•
Incandescent to fluorescent lighting options
•
Time of day schedules with time clocks
•
Ride the pump/fan/compressor curves
•
Rudimentary energy usage reporting
•
Simplistic tower controls
•
Demand-limiting control algorithms
•
Advanced VAV systems with optimized controls
•
Advanced airside control algorithms such as critical zone reset
•
0.48 or even 0.45 kW/ton chillers and other high efficiency
products
•
Quicker and easier to use energy analysis tools
•
Pre-engineered thermal energy storage systems
•
Properly balanced outside air using control algorithms based
on ASHRAE guidelines
•
High-efficiency fluorescent lighting and ballasts
•
Occupancy sensors
•
Optimal start/stop control algorithms
•
Night setback/setup control algorithms
•
Hybrid plants to use more available energy sources
•
Variable-speed pumping
•
Low-temperature air and water distribution
•
Variable-frequency drives
•
Energy aggregation/analysis/brokerage
•
Chiller/tower optimization
•
Focus on “total cost of ownership” with special attention to
sustainability through improved service
States.3 Our challenge is to make sure
building owners, designers, contractors
and manufacturers work together to de-
velop the best solution for each specific
application. Being aware of these oppor-
tunities is the first step.
Environmental Considerations
The U.S. Environmental Protection
Agency (EPA) has several tools to help
make building owners and managers aware
of energy efficiency and environment-
friendly options. Programs under the “En-
ergy Star Buildings” initiative are examples.
The EPA is encouraging building owners
to take a holistic approach to building en-
ergy and environmental issues. For in-
stance, a recent initiative, “Building Air
Conditioner Climate Partnership”4 encour-
ages focus on all emissions that may re-
sult from maintaining comfort in a build-
ing. Specifically, it encourages owners and
system designers not to focus on the
President Carter urged Americans to
turn down their thermostats during the
last energy crisis.
42
ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
May 2001
ASHRAE Journal
chemicals in the products. They should focus on the emissions
that are generated not only from the products but also from the
power plants that generate the electricity used by the products.
This approach allows designers, contractors and manufacturers
to leverage all the tools available to find the optimum energy and
environmental solutions for building owners.
For example, the utility generated emissions typically repre-
sent more than 96% of all of the life-cycle emissions for products
such as large water chillers. This illustrates why, from an environ-
mental perspective, the focus should be on efficiency. The leak-
age of refrigerant is important as it affects refrigerant emissions
and the operating efficiency of the chiller.
As discussed earlier, the HVAC industry has made substan-
tial progress in the past 20 years in many areas. A specific
example of the improvement in equipment efficiency is that of
large water chillers, Table 3.
The impact of this efficiency improvement on the environment
is significant. As a basis of comparison, for every 0.1 kW/ton
improvement of all centrifugal chillers the annual power plant
emissions would be reduced by 21 billion pounds (9.5 billion kg)

of CO2, 6.3 billion grams (13 million lbs) of SO2 and 3.5 billion
grams of NOX (8 million lbs). This is equivalent to removing
nearly three million cars from the road or planting 600 million
trees each year.
This efficiency, which is becoming so important today, will
become even more important tomorrow. It is also important that
the equipment used in HVAC applications meet environmental
expectations for proper design and usage of refrigerants and
other substances.
This was made clear in a United Nations statement released
at the Kyoto Protocol Meeting in December 1997:5 “It (the Pro-
tocol) creates new incentives for technological creativity and
the adoption of ‘no-regrets’ solutions that make economic
and environmental sense irrespective of climate change. Be-
cause activities and products with zero or low emissions will
gain competitive advantage, the energy, transport, industrial,
housing and agricultural sectors will gradually move towards
more climate-friendly technologies and practices.”
Again, a direct connection exists between energy conserva-
tion and sound environmental practices. As an industry, we
have the products and tools available to meet these needs.
Sustaining the Savings
It is also critical not only to design and install efficient sys-
tems. It is also critical to maintain them to sustain high efficiency
during the entire life of the building or process. Proactively moni-
toring, re-commissioning and servicing building systems will re-
sult in maintaining or even improving the energy efficiency as
building use changes.
Many tools are available to servicing technicians and engi-
neers to determine when equipment needs servicing. Rather than
relying on a “fix-on-fail” or pre-scheduled inspections and ser-
vice, we can proactively monitor the actual performance of equip-
ment and systems and provide maintenance that increases reli-
ability, up-time and maintains high-energy efficiency.
Figure 1: Investment options.
Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.
May 2001
ASHRAE Journal
43
Energy
Financial Considerations
Another area to consider is financing
options that help building owners invest
in high-efficiency systems. There has been
progress in new financial vehicles since
the late 1970s. These financial instruments
allow building owners to use the opera-
tional savings generated from energy effi-
ciency and conservation to fund the in-
cremental increase in first cost that is of-
ten needed to purchase the higher effi-
ciency products, systems and services.
Some of the more common tools include
capital and operating leases, performance-
based contracting and comprehensive
maintenance agreements. Sometimes these
are combined with a portion of capital ex-
penditures and the financing is only of the
incremental cost between standard and
high-performance systems. Obviously,
each business and building owner will have
unique financial needs that need to be con-
sidered. However, the range and availabil-
ity of financial tools is greater today than
ever and first cost should no longer be the
barrier to high efficiency.
From a financial investment perspective,
the risk/reward ratio of energy conserva-
tion projects is relatively low. Figure 1,
from the U.S. EPA (with data taken from
Richards, et al.) illustrates this. As busi-
ness leaders weigh the choices they have
for investments, it is important to help them
understand the high probability of achiev-
ing solid returns from investing in energy
conservation measures.
Let’s Act Now
The HVAC and controls industry mo-
bilized to effectively respond to the en-
ergy crisis nearly two decades ago. To-
day, we have the tools, knowledge and
products to help our customers and the
country respond to the challenges pre-
sented by the current energy situation
and ensure the efficiency gains are sus-
tainable during the entire life of the build-
ing or process. Handled properly, con-
servation in buildings can improve finan-
cial performance for building owners, pro-
vide better working environments
through improved HVAC and lighting
systems and reduce overall environmen-
tal emissions through the use of highly
efficient system. Potentially, the new en-
ergy awareness—or even crisis—is a win-
win situation. Let’s increase the respect
afforded the HVAC industry by applying
our knowledge to once again act hero-
ically to do “the right things” for our cus-
tomers and our country.

February.

ing, January.
5.United Nations press release issued on
December 11, 1997.

ASHRAE Journal
Chillers
14
ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
May 2001
F
Improving the Efficiency
Of Chilled Water Plants
About the Author
By Gil Avery, P.E.
Fellow ASHRAE
or the past 30 or more years most central chilled water plants have
used primary-secondary pumping arrangements as shown in
Figure 1. The primary system includes all equipment to the plant side
of Points A and B and the secondary system includes all equipment to the
load side of these points. The majority of these systems have the chillers
staged from flow. As the flow in the secondary starts to exceed the flow in
the primary, an additional primary pump and chiller is staged on. As the flow
in the secondary decreases, so that one less chiller can handle the reduced
flow, a pump and chiller is staged off. This method of control assumes that
the load is proportional to flow and that the ∆T between the chilled water
supply and return is constant and at the design condition.
These assumptions are generally
wrong. Systems rarely operate at design
∆T because of coils that are dirty, inad-
equate coil control valves, thermostats
that are set wrong, excessive temperature
drops in the supply and return mains, etc.
Therefore, flow is not a reliable indica-
tion of load. As a result, the vast majority
of central chilled water plants operate in-
efficiently with lower than design ∆Ts and
with more partially loaded chillers on-line
than would be necessary if the chillers
were properly staged.
The Way It’s Been Done
The typical primary/secondary system
shown in Figure 1 is fully loaded at the
outdoor design conditions of 95°F (35°C)
dry bulb and 78°F (25.5°C) wet bulb. The
system is operating as designed with
6,000 gpm (379 L/s) of 45°F (7°C) supply
water and 57°F (13.8°C) return water (2
gpm/ton [0.03 mL/J]). Each of the three
1,000 ton (3517 kW) chillers and compan-
ion pumps are handling 2,000 gpm
(126 L/s). The fourth standby pump is off.
In Figure 2 the load is only 600 tons
(2110 kW). The ∆T is 6°F (3.3°C) (4
gpm/ton [0.07 mL/J]), which is typical of
many systems designed to operate at a
higher ∆T. Two pumps and chillers are
on-line, with each chiller inefficiently
loaded to 300 tons (1055 kW); 4,000 gpm
(252 L/s) is circulated in the primary, 1,600
gpm (101 L/s) through the bypass and
2,400 gpm (151 L/s) (600 tons × 4 gpm/ton
[2110 kW × 0.07 mL/J) in the secondary. If
the system were properly staged and
piped, one chiller could easily handle the
600 ton (2110 kW) load.
In Figure 3 the load has increased to
1,500 tons (5275 kW); 6,000 gpm (379 L/s)
is circulating in the primary and second-
ary systems with no flow in the bypass.
Three inefficiently loaded chillers and
three pumps are on-line. Two chillers
could handle the load.
To satisfy the 1,600 ton (5627 kW) load
in Figure 4, 6,400 gpm (404 L/s) (1,600 × 4
gpm/ton [5627 × 0.07 mL/J]) is required.
The flow in the secondary now exceeds
the primary flow of 6,000 gpm (379 L/s),
and return water through the bypass
starts to mix with the supply water. This
raises the supply water temperature and
the air-handling unit control valves start
to open, thus increasing both the sec-
ondary and bypass flow. Temperature
control in the conditioned spaces is lost,
even though only about half of the cen-
tral plant capacity is used. When chillers
are staged from flow and are controlled at
the design leaving chilled water tempera-
ture, the approximate maximum system
capacity is:
(
)
(
)
T
T
∆
×
∆
Design
Capacity
Design
Operating
Very often facilities with operating con-
ditions such as these will add an additional
chiller and pump even though the existing
plant would have sufficient capacity if the
chillers were piped and staged properly.
Adding more equipment is not the solu-
tion. The secondary pump and piping is
not designed for the additional flow.
The capacity of a chiller, using hydro-
carbon-based refrigerant, increases from
0.5% to 1.5% for every degree decrease in
entering condenser water temperature.
However, the increase in chiller capacity
at a lower kW/ton cannot be used when
chillers are staged from flow. Whenever
the flow exceeds the design flow of the
chiller in the typical primary/secondary
system, the next machine is staged on even
though the operating machine(s) are not
fully loaded. The maximum capacity of any
machine never goes above the 100% ca-
pacity abscissa line in Figure 5. Most chill-
ers are rated at 85°F (29°C) entering con-
denser water temperature, a condition that
may exist less than 2% of the year. At other
times whenever the outdoor wet bulb and
Gil Avery, P.E., is a principal in The
Kele Companies in Memphis, Tenn.
May 2001
ASHRAE Journal
15
Chillers
entering condenser water temperature is less than design, the
chiller capacity will increase and the kW/ton will decrease. This
higher efficiency and lower kW/ton operating range is in the
shaded area above the 100% capacity abscissa line in Figure 5.
A high-efficiency chiller may operate at 0.55 kW/ton with 85°F
(29°C) condenser water and at 0.34 kW/ton with 55°F (12.7°C)
condenser water. Most 1,000 ton (3517 kW) chillers may there-
fore produce more than 1,100 tons (3869 kW) when the entering
condenser water temperature is 65°F–70°F (18°C–21°C).
The majority of primary/secondary chilled water plants oper-
ate similar to those depicted in Figures 2 and 3, with too many
chillers on-line for efficient operation. Minimizing the number
of chillers on-line reduces the plant kW/ton since the unneeded
pumps and auxiliary devices are also off-line.
Improving The Efficiency
Although the remedy is relatively simple and inexpensive,
operators are hesitant to change, thinking, “That was the way
the system was designed to operate.” Initially everything pos-
sible must be done to correct the issues contributing to the low
∆T. Then change the way the chillers are staged on and off and
install a check valve in the bypass line at Point C as shown in
Figure 4 to prevent return water from mixing with supply water.
The check valve and bypass line should be sized to handle the
minimum flow of the largest chiller. A non-slamming check valve
with a manual opening device is generally used. A temperature
sensor in the secondary supply water, set low enough to sat-
isfy the load requiring the coldest water, will stage the chillers
on. They are staged off by another sensor in the secondary
Figure 1: A typical primary/secondary chiller system fully
loaded at the outdoor design conditions of 95°F (35°C) dry
bulb and 78°F (25.5°C) wet bulb.
Figure 2: A typical primary/secondary chiller system with
a load of only 600 tons (2110 kW).
Figure 3: A typical primary/secondary chiller system with
the load having increased to 1,500 tons (5275 kW).
Figure 4: A typical primary/secondary chiller system with
a 1,600 ton (5627 kW) load.
16
ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
May 2001
ASHRAE Journal
return water and by measuring the load (flow × ∆T). When the
load decreases below the capacity of an on-line chiller, that
chiller and pump is staged off. The sensors in the supply and
return lines must be matched sensors with an accuracy of
±0.10°F (0.5°C). These changes generally have been shown to
reduce the kW/ton by 10 to 20%.
Most chillers will operate satisfactorily with evaporator tube
water velocities between 3 and 12 fps (0.9 and 3.6 m/s). Under
certain conditions (e.g., startup), the flow in the secondary will
exceed the primary design flow and the primary and secondary
pumps will then be in series. There will be a slight increase in
the flow through the chillers under these conditions but since
the flow varies as the square of the head, the actual increase
will rarely exceed 10% above design. An increase in flow even
this great would require an increase in pump head of over 20%
a condition unlikely to occur with the usual centrifugal pumps
that are chosen with flat pump curves.
Single Pump Primary/Secondary System
For new construction, the central plant can be designed us-
ing a single pump, as shown in Figure 6. Conventional primary
pumps are usually large, low-speed, low-head pumps that re-
quire an excessive amount of floor space. Their elimination can
materially reduce the initial cost of the system.
The differential pressure sensor “DP-1” controls bypass
valve “V-4” to maintain a minimum differential (minimum flow)
across the chiller evaporators. When a chiller is staged on and
the differential pressure decreases, the processor will modulate
valve “V-4” open. When the coil valves or bypass valve “V-4”
opens, the differential pressure at sensor “DP-2” will decrease
and the processor will ramp up the speed of pump “P-1” through
the drive “VFD” to maintain a constant differential across the
ends of the supply and return mains. Flow at sensor “FS-1” and
temperature at sensors “T-1” and “T-2” are used by the proces-
sor to stage the chillers on and off using the sequence de-
scribed previously. Valves “V-1,” “V-2,” and “V-3” are closed
when their respective chiller is off. Similar controls have been
applied for many years to constant flow systems that were
converted to variable flow and only one pump was available.
Although flow sensor “FS-1” is shown, the differential pres-
Figure 7: Multi-plexed sources and loads.
Figure 6: A single pump primary–secondary chilled water system.
Figure 5: Percent of rated capacity vs. outdoor wet-
bulb temperature.
May 2001
ASHRAE Journal
17
sure measured by “DP-1” across the chiller evaporators can be
used to calculate the flow.
The accuracy of this method of calculating flow increases in
proportion to the number of chillers in the central plant. Each
evaporator circuit acts as an orifice. As long as the system is
closed to the atmosphere, there will not be a significant change
in pressure drop through the evaporator tubes as the system
ages. On plants with three or more chillers, the accuracy ap-
proaches that of most flow sensors.
The bypass valve “V-4” and bypass line should be sized to
handle the minimum flow of the largest chiller. The drop across
the bypass and valve must be less than the minimum operating
differential (setting of “DP-2”). Valve “V-4” must be coupled to
an actuator that can close off against the full head of the sec-
ondary system. Correctly sized industrial metal-seated butter-
fly or ball valves are a good choice if they are selected to with-
stand the system dynamic forces.
Decentralized Systems
Outlying buildings on campuses and institutional complexes
may have chilled water sources that can be connected to the
chilled water system through the supply and return manifold
as shown in Figure 7. Building A houses the chilled water or
ice storage facility. Building B has an existing chiller and load.
Buildings C and E have a load and source respectively. Build-
ing D houses the central chiller plant. All sources pump out of
the return manifold through the cooling source and into the
supply manifold. All loads are pumped out of the supply mani-
fold through the load and into the return manifold. Two posi-
tion valves “V-1” are used to isolate inactive loads and sources.
Multiplexing all of the chilled water sources and loads in this
manner has many advantages. Any source or combination of
sources can serve any load or combination of loads any place
on the manifold. Loads and sources can be matched to provide
the most efficient operation. Manifold piping is smaller and
less expensive than conventional loop piping. Chiller redun-
dancy is maximized. A detailed method of controlling loads and
sources is found in (Avery 1987).
Tertiary Pumping Systems
Tertiary loads on central systems are generally piped in the
customary fashion as shown in Figure 8. Temperature sensor
“T-1” controls valve “V-1” to maintain the design supply chilled
water temperature. Differential pressure sensor “DP-1” con-
Figure 8: Tertiary pumping system.
Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.
18
ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
May 2001
ASHRAE Journal
Figure 9: Tertiary booster pumping system.
trols the speed of the building pump through the variable fre-
quency drive “VFD.” This method of piping and control does
not take advantage of the differential pressure that is available
between the supply and return mains. The pressure is wasted
across valve “V-1.” A preferred method of piping the tertiary
system is shown in Figure 9. The differential pressure sensor
“DP-1” controls valve “V-1” in sequence with the speed of the
pump through the variable frequency drive “VFD.” As the load
in the building increases, valve “V-1” modulates open. On a
continued drop in differential pressure, pump “P-1” will start
and ramp up to the speed required to maintain the design differ-
ential pressure at “DP-1.” The cycle reverses on a rise in differ-
ential pressure. Using the pressure between the supply and
return mains reduces the building pumping energy.
Summary
The use of the typical primary/secondary paradigm shown in
Figure 1 may be costing building owners millions of dollars in
additional operating costs because of the inefficient nature of
these systems. There are better ways of designing chilled water
systems. It’s time to get our heads out of the 1950s and start
using what we’ve learned since then.
Bibliography
Avery, G. 1987. “Microprocessor control for large chilled water
distribution systems.” Heating/Piping/Air Conditioning Engineering.
Avery, G. 1993. “Designing and commissioning variable flow hy-
dronic systems.” ASHRAE Journal 35(7):45–49.
Avery. G. 1998. “Controlling chillers in variable flow systems.”
ASHRAE Journal 40(2):42–45.
Avery, G. 2000. “Selecting valves and piping coils.” ASHRAE Jour-
nal 42(4):23–27.

ASHRAE Journal
Outdoor Air
28
ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
May 2001
I
Designing Dedicated
Outdoor Air Systems
About the Author
By Stanley A. Mumma, Ph.D., P.E.
Fellow ASHRAE
Stanley A. Mumma, Ph.D., P.E., is a
professor at Penn State University, Uni-
versity Park, Pa. He serves on
ASHRAE Technical Committees, Inte-
grated Building Design and Solar En-
ergy Utilization.
ntegration of dedicated outdoor air (OA) systems with parallel terminal
systems is not new.1 However, many engineers have dismissed the con-
cept of a separate dedicated OA system on the basis of cost and space
limitations. Recently, some industry leaders2,3 have employed designs using
the dedicated OA approach. The author’s paper in ASHRAE Transac-
tions4 illustrates the complexity of the problem and raises serious questions
about the ability of all-air VAV systems to actually meet the ventilation rate
procedure of ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62-1999, Ventilation for Accept-
able Indoor Air Quality.
low as required by ANSI/ASHRAE/
IESNA Standard 90.1-1999, Energy Stan-
dard for Buildings Except Low-Rise Resi-
dential Buildings, the primary source of
moisture often is the occupant.
Because the dedicated OA system is
designed to deliver the required ventila-
tion air to each occupant (15 to 20 scfm/
person [7 to 9 L/s per person]), the
change in humidity ratio of the ventila-
tion air is easily computed for any given
activity level. Typically, this requires a
supply air dew-point temperature of about
45°F (7°C) to maintain a space dew-point
temperature around 52°F (11°C) (support
for this lower than customary space dew-
point temperature follows).
Dehumidification and Cooling
Dehumidification can be accomplished
using either active desiccants (solid or liq-
uid) or cooling coils. As a general rule,
cooling coils are a better choice when the
required dew-point temperature is above
40°F (4°C). Active desiccants are a better
choice when the dew-point temperature is
below 40°F (4°C). Therefore, only cooling-
based dehumidification is explored here.
Properly selected and maintained deep
A separate dedicated OA ventilation
system may be the only reliable method
of meeting Standard 62-1999. It is also the
simplest method. The separate dedicated
OA concept can be expanded, if the air is
properly conditioned, to completely meet
the space latent loads, thus decoupling
the space latent and sensible loads.
Therefore, a new design/integration para-
digm was developed with the following
two basic tenets:
1. Employ a separate dedicated outdoor
air system to deliver the proper ventila-
tion air to each individual space and to
remove all of the outdoor air and space
latent cooling loads.
2. Remove the space sensible cooling
loads, primarily independent of the dedi-
cated outdoor air system, with a parallel
mechanical system.
OA Supply Temperature and
Dew-Point Temperature
Decoupling the space sensible and la-
tent loads requires that the ventilation air
be supplied with a sufficiently low hu-
midity ratio to remove the moisture gen-
erated within the space, as well as that
carried in by infiltration. If infiltration is
chilled-water cooling coils, capable of pro-
ducing the approximately 45°F (7°C) dew-
point temperature, appear to be the best
choice. The coil capacity can be modu-
lated to match the transient load condi-
tions. Also, off peak air conditioning (ice
or water thermal storage) can be used.
Reheat
In the event that the required supply
air dew-point temperature is lower than
the desired supply air dry-bulb tempera-
ture, reheat is necessary.
Winter Humidification
Winter humidification often is over-
looked in current design practice. How-
ever, since it is recommended that the win-
ter space relative humidity (RH) be main-
tained above 40% for optimal thermal com-
fort, and for minimizing microbial-related
health risks,5 humidification is important.
The significant OA flow rates required
by Standard 62-1999 can lead to a very
low wintertime indoor air relative humid-
ity in many locations when humidifica-
tion is not used.
Equipment Configurations
The three configurations presented in
Figure 1 are discussed in this article. In
Configuration 1, free reheat is achieved
with the runaround heat recovery coils
surrounding the cooling coil. A heating
coil is available to temper the supply air
when there is insufficient heat in the in-
coming outdoor air to complete the job,
and for winter conditions. A humidifier is
May 2001
ASHRAE Journal
29
Outdoor Air
used to provide winter humidification.
In Configuration 2, an enthalpy wheel is used to precondition
the OA ahead of the cooling coil. The enthalpy wheel cools and
dehumidifies the outdoor air in the summer, lowering the load on
the cooling coil. In the winter, the enthalpy wheel can be used to
heat and humidify the outdoor air, eliminating the need for the
humidifier. Preheating is required to prevent frost formation on
the wheel. The runaround heat recovery coils and heating coil
perform essentially the same duty as in Configuration 1.
Configuration 3 is similar to Configuration 2, but with the
runaround coils replaced with a sensible heat recovery wheel
for reheat. The sensible wheel completely eliminates the need
for the heating coil found in Configuration 2.6
Simulated Performance for the Three Configurations
Mathematical models and control algorithms were written for
each of the configurations.6 Atlanta’s typical meteorological
year (TMY) data was used to determine the peak design com-
ponent loads and the annual component energy consumption.
The simulation used 3,744 hours per year occupancy and 10,000
scfm (4719 L/s) of OA. The results are summarized in Table 1.
Based upon the results of the simulations presented in Table
1, the best configuration for dedicated outdoor air systems
intended to decouple the space latent and sensible loads is
Configuration 3. From this point on, Configuration 3 shall be
referred to as dedicated outdoor air system (DOAS).
Operation of the DOAS
The operation of the DOAS is best understood with the help
of a psychrometric chart. The psychrometric chart in Figure 2
(with Atlanta weather data plotted) shows the four regions: A,
B, C, and D where the OA may fall. The white lines are based
upon the following conditions:
• State 3, 45°F (7°C) and saturated;
• State 4, 55°F (13°C) dry-bulb temperature and 45°F (7°C)
dew-point temperature;
• State 5, 80°F (27°C) dry-bulb temperature and 55°F (13°C)
dew-point temperature; and
• State 6, 70°F (21°C) dry-bulb temperature and 55°F (13°C)
dew-point temperature.
A horizontal line represents 45°F (7°C) dew-point tempera-
ture, the supply air dew-point temperature. If the outside air
conditions fall above that line, the air must be cooled and dehu-
midified to State 3 and then reheated to State 4 with the sensible
wheel. The sensible cooling of the relief air from State 5 to State
6 is a result of energy extraction from the return air. An identical
rate of heat is added to the supply air leaving the deep cooling
coil (CC) at State 3, reheating it to State 4.
A line of constant enthalpy passing through State 6 sepa-
rates the area above the 45°F (7°C) dew-point temperature line
into Regions A and B. The boundary between Regions A and B
(h6) separates OA conditions, where dehumidification is re-
quired, into the two regions. In Region A, full use of the en-
thalpy wheel dramatically reduces the cooling coil load. In Re-
gion B, any use of the enthalpy wheel increases the cooling coil
load. Therefore, the enthalpy wheel must be off.
Another boundary is formed by the extension of a line through
the return condition State 5 and the supply State 4. The line,
which first appears at State 4 and proceeds to a humidity ratio of
0 gr/lb (0 g), divides the area below the 45°F (7°C) dew-point
temperature line into Regions C and D. The boundary between
Regions C and D separates the OA conditions, where humidifica-
tion is required, into two regions. In region C, sensible cooling is
required. In Region D, no sensible cooling is required. The equip-
ment operating status is presented in Table 2.

ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org

ASHRAE Journal
Selecting Supply Air Conditions for the DOAS
Given that most decisions are based on first-cost consider-
ations, and then on energy costs, the following hypotheses
must be tested when selecting the supply air conditions:
• Hypothesis 1: low supply air temperatures will reduce the
parallel equipment load, and hence their first costs and energy
transport costs. The supply air temperature could be as low as
the required supply air dew-point temperature and as high as
neutral.
• Hypothesis 2: lower supply air dew-point temperature will
result in lower space dew-point temperature, allowing the paral-
lel terminal equipment to operate at lower temperatures, further
reducing the first costs and energy transport costs.
• Hypothesis 3: supply air temperatures of 55°F (13°C) will
result in no more terminal reheat, and frequently less than is
currently required with conventional VAV systems using box
minimums as dictated by Standard 62-1999.
These hypotheses have been tested7 and found to be true.
The conclusion from that work is “select the supply air dew-
point temperature low enough to maintain a summer space RH
no greater than 40%, or a supply air dew-point temperature
around 44°F (7°C). Likewise, the supply air dry-bulb tempera-
ture should be at or below 55°F (13°C).”
Meeting the Space Cooling Loads
Many options are available to meet the sensible cooling load
not met by the 55°F (13°C) or colder ventilation air from the DOAS
equipment. One option is ceiling radiant cooling panels.8
Some of the advantages of ceiling radiant cooling panels are:
• First cost (with experienced contractors) is about 15% less
than installing a conventional air system;
• Long-term savings are dramatic, (i.e., approximately 20%
to 30%, as a result of reduced fan power);
• The panels provide reduced operation and maintenance
costs (minimal moving parts and no filters);
• Testing and balancing during commissioning before occu-
pancy is simpler and less expensive to perform;
• Comfort levels can be better than those of other condition-
ing systems because radiant loads are treated directly and air
motion in the space is at normal ventilation levels;
• Supply air quantities usually do not exceed those required
for ventilation and dehumidification;
• A 100% outdoor air system may be installed with smaller
penalties, in terms of refrigeration load, because of reduced
outdoor air quantities (multiple spaces equation 6.1 of Stan-
dard 62-1999 does not apply to this situation);
• Wet surface cooling coils are eliminated from the occupied
space, reducing the potential for septic contamination;
• The panel system can use the automatic sprinkler system
piping (see NFPA Standard 13, Chapter 3, Section 3.6);
• A compact design;
• Vertical shaft space area/volume savings;
• A quick response to load dynamics; and
• Zoning is easily achieved, and can accommodate chang-
ing office scapes.
Ceiling cooling panels remove heat from the space by radia-
tion and convection. For normal room and panel operating tem-
peratures, the panel can remove 30 Btu/h·ft2 (95 W/m2) (about 15
Btu/h·ft2 [47 W/m2] by radiation and about 15Btu/h·ft2 [47 W/m2]
by convection). This rate of heat removal can be placed in per-
spective with a few approximations. If one assumes that a typical
VAV system delivering 55°F (13°C) air will supply 1 cfm/ft2, such
a system has the capability of removing 21.6 Btu/h·ft2 (68 W/m2)
of sensible heat. If it also is assumed that the ventilation air
supply is about 0.2 cfm/ft2 and supplied at 55°F (13°C), the sen-
sible load remaining is 80% of the 21.6 Btu/hr·ft2 (68 W/m2), or
slightly more than 17 Btu/hr·ft2 (54 W/m2). Under these circum-
stances, the panel area to ceiling area ratio is 17/30, or less than
60% of the ceiling requires cooling panels. The percentage of
ceiling used for cooling panels drops to about 50% when the
ventilation supply air temperature drops to 45°F (7°C). A poten-
tial schematic of the thermal transport system-fire suppression
transport system integration with the DOAS cooling coil and the
chiller is shown and discussed in detail in 2001 ASHRAE Trans-
actions.9
Conclusions and Recommendations
The paradigm shift in the design of building comfort control
systems is in its early stages. The requirements of the new
paradigm include:
• Separating the OA system from the space-conditioning
systems to ensure proper ventilation in all occupied spaces,
• Conditioning the OA to handle all of the space latent load
and as much of the space sensible load as is economically
feasible without excessive reheat,
• Maximizing the cost-effective use of energy recovery
equipment,
• Integrating the fire suppression and energy transport sys-
tems, and
• Using ceiling radiant sensible cooling panels for occupant
thermal control where appropriate.
To facilitate transition the to the new paradigm, the following
near-term goals must be achieved.
1. First, the paradigm must be implemented into buildings
located in each of the four major quadrants of the United States.
2. Second, a professional development program must be cre-
ated and deployed around the country.
3. Third, the U.S. ceiling radiant cooling panel industry must
be developed/gear up to meet the potential $5 billion/year to
$50 billion/year business opportunity.
